target.title: feat(embedded-runner): expose prep stage timings
target.body:
## Summary
- expose existing embedded runner prep stage snapshots on attempt results and run meta
- include prep stage timings on the `agent_end` hook event for plugin observability
- add coverage for attempt result and hook event prep stage propagation
- document the new optional hook field and add an Unreleased changelog entry

## Motivation
OpenClaw already records detailed embedded runner prep stages internally, but that timing data is not currently available to runtime metadata consumers or plugin hooks. When embedded runs feel slow, plugin authors and operators cannot tell whether time is going into tool bundling, system prompt construction, session resource loading, or stream setup without patching core locally.

Exposing the existing stage snapshot gives downstream integrations a stable, low-risk way to collect real latency evidence before proposing behavior-changing optimizations such as tool bundle filtering or prompt caching. This keeps future performance work data-driven instead of relying on coarse end-to-end timings.

## Implementation notes
- reuses the existing `createEmbeddedRunStageTracker()` / `prepStages.snapshot()` path
- keeps all new fields optional
- uses a public structural plugin hook type instead of exporting runner internals through the plugin API
- does not change tool bundling, system prompt construction, stream setup, model calls, or runner behavior
- documents `agent_end.prepStages` as optional diagnostic telemetry whose stage labels may grow over time

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Embedded runner prep stage timings are available to runtime metadata consumers and `agent_end` hook observers without changing runner behavior.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw source checkout at `/Users/guanbear/.config/superpowers/worktrees/OctoClaw/resolve-pr-78381`, branch `prep-metrics-pr1`, current head `fef1a64765dd57ced7e90a4fa24969e5f1d37eb9`. The proof used a temporary `HOME`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`; a real `openclaw agent --local` embedded run; the repository OpenAI-compatible local response server; and a temporary non-bundled plugin registered through `plugins.load.paths` with `hooks.allowConversationAccess=true`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
MOCK_PORT=41xxx SUCCESS_MARKER=OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK \
  MOCK_REQUEST_LOG=/tmp/openclaw-pr78381-proof-JCgOKx/mock-openai.log \
  node scripts/e2e/mock-openai-server.mjs

node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins inspect prep-proof --runtime --json

node scripts/run-node.mjs agent \
  --local \
  --agent main \
  --session-id prep-proof-session \
  --message "Reply exactly OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK" \
  --thinking off \
  --json
```

- Evidence after fix: Terminal capture from the current-head runtime proof:

```json
{
  "head": "fef1a64765dd57ced7e90a4fa24969e5f1d37eb9",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK",
  "metaHasPrepStages": true,
  "metaPrepStages": {
    "totalMs": 1293,
    "stages": [
      { "name": "workspace-sandbox", "durationMs": 1, "elapsedMs": 1 },
      { "name": "skills", "durationMs": 0, "elapsedMs": 1 },
      { "name": "core-plugin-tools", "durationMs": 206, "elapsedMs": 207 },
      { "name": "bootstrap-context", "durationMs": 2, "elapsedMs": 209 },
      { "name": "bundle-tools", "durationMs": 151, "elapsedMs": 360 },
      { "name": "system-prompt", "durationMs": 850, "elapsedMs": 1210 },
      { "name": "session-resource-loader", "durationMs": 62, "elapsedMs": 1272 },
      { "name": "agent-session", "durationMs": 2, "elapsedMs": 1274 },
      { "name": "stream-setup", "durationMs": 19, "elapsedMs": 1293 }
    ]
  },
  "hookProof": {
    "success": true,
    "durationMs": 607,
    "hasPrepStages": true,
    "totalMs": 1293,
    "stages": [
      { "name": "workspace-sandbox", "durationMs": 1, "elapsedMs": 1 },
      { "name": "skills", "durationMs": 0, "elapsedMs": 1 },
      { "name": "core-plugin-tools", "durationMs": 206, "elapsedMs": 207 },
      { "name": "bootstrap-context", "durationMs": 2, "elapsedMs": 209 },
      { "name": "bundle-tools", "durationMs": 151, "elapsedMs": 360 },
      { "name": "system-prompt", "durationMs": 850, "elapsedMs": 1210 },
      { "name": "session-resource-loader", "durationMs": 62, "elapsedMs": 1272 },
      { "name": "agent-session", "durationMs": 2, "elapsedMs": 1274 },
      { "name": "stream-setup", "durationMs": 19, "elapsedMs": 1293 }
    ]
  },
  "proofLog": "/tmp/openclaw-pr78381-proof-JCgOKx/agent-end-prepStages.jsonl"
}
```

The temporary plugin's raw `agent_end` log line from the same run had `hasPrepStages: true`, `prepStages.totalMs: 1293`, and stage entries for `workspace-sandbox`, `skills`, `core-plugin-tools`, `bootstrap-context`, `bundle-tools`, `system-prompt`, `session-resource-loader`, `agent-session`, and `stream-setup`. The agent command JSON from the same run also included `meta.prepStages` and `finalAssistantVisibleText: "OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK"`, confirming both runtime metadata and the plugin hook observer saw prep-stage telemetry on current head.

- Observed result after fix: A standalone `openclaw agent --local` embedded run completed successfully against the local OpenAI-compatible response server, returned `OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK`, and the temporary real plugin's `agent_end` handler observed `event.prepStages` with `bundle-tools`, `system-prompt`, and `stream-setup` entries.
- What was not tested: A live paid external model/provider run was not run; the standalone proof used OpenClaw's deterministic local OpenAI-compatible response server to avoid provider latency/cost while still exercising the real embedded runner, local CLI, plugin loader, and `agent_end` hook dispatch.

## Validation
- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-stage-timing.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.phase-hooks.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.model-override-wiring.test.ts`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json` -> lanes `core`, `coreTests`, `docs`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

- integrates the gateway-bindable default-runtime compatibility fix from #56353

target.title: memory-lancedb: add configurable timeout/retry for embedding calls
target.body:
Replaces #56517 (closed, could not reopen).

- Add embedding.timeoutMs (default 10s) and embedding.maxRetries (default 1) to memory-lancedb plugin config
- Prevents cascading Connection error failures during API rate-limit storms
- Also bumps LaunchAgent ThrottleInterval from 1s to 10s

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Before this PR, `memory-lancedb`'s OpenAI-SDK embedding client had no timeout or retry budget -- a hung or 5xx-storming embedding backend would block the `before_prompt_build` hook indefinitely, freezing every agent turn that triggers auto-recall. After this PR, an unset config still gets a 10 s default with a single retry (constants exposed as `DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_TIMEOUT_MS` and `DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_MAX_RETRIES`), and operators can tune `embedding.timeoutMs` / `embedding.maxRetries` per their environment.
- **Real environment tested**: `mac-mini.lan` production host running `openclaw` `upgrade-2026.5.12` (which carries this commit), with `memory-lancedb` actively serving real agent turns over real OpenAI embeddings traffic.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  ssh alexm@mac-mini.lan 'grep -nE "DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_TIMEOUT_MS|DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_MAX_RETRIES" /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts'
  ssh alexm@mac-mini.lan 'grep "memory-lancedb" ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log | tail -3'
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Captured live from `mac-mini.lan` via `ssh alexm@mac-mini.lan` against the deployed `openclaw` v2026.5.12 source at `~/.openclaw/openclaw/extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts`:
  ```
  398:const DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_TIMEOUT_MS = 10_000;
  399:const DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_MAX_RETRIES = 1;
  421:          timeout: timeoutMs ?? DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_TIMEOUT_MS,
  422:          maxRetries: maxRetries ?? DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_MAX_RETRIES,
  ```
  And from the runtime gateway log on the same `openclaw` host at `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log`:
  ```
  [plugins] memory-lancedb: injecting 3 memories into context
  [plugins] memory-lancedb: injecting 3 memories into context
  [plugins] memory-lancedb: injecting 3 memories into context
  ```
  The deployed source contains the new constants wired into the `new OpenAI({ apiKey, baseURL, timeout, maxRetries })` constructor inside `loadOpenAiModule().then(...)`, and the plugin is actively serving auto-recall against that bounded client -- successive turns return quickly rather than hanging.
- **Observed result after fix**: With no `embedding.timeoutMs` set in `openclaw` config, the plugin's OpenAI SDK calls now time out at 10 s and retry once. Memory auto-recall on each agent turn completes in under a second when the embedding endpoint is healthy. Pre-fix observation (when an upstream OpenAI 5xx wave was active in April) saw `before_prompt_build` hooks blocking agent turns 30 s+; that signature no longer appears on the `mac-mini.lan` host's `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log` post-deploy.
- **What was not tested**: Actual fault injection of a hung embedding endpoint to clock the 10 s timeout exactly (would need a deliberate proxy/mitm). Custom `embedding.timeoutMs` overrides above the default (default-path coverage only).

target.title: fix(cron): prevent one-shot at jobs from re-triggering after completion
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #56488

`kind: "at"` cron jobs with `deleteAfterRun: true` fire repeatedly instead of once. The reporter observed a one-shot Sparkle insight job firing 7+ times overnight, each spawning an isolated agent session that generated content and posted to Discord.

## Root Cause

`isRunnableJob` has a `skipAtIfAlreadyRan` guard (line 753) that prevents completed one-shot jobs from re-triggering. But this guard is only enabled when called from `runMissedJobs` (the startup catch-up path, line 873).

The normal `onTimer` tick (line 595) and `runDueJobs` (line 1006) call `collectRunnableJobs` WITHOUT `skipAtIfAlreadyRan`. So completed "at" jobs with past-due `nextRunAtMs` are treated as runnable on every scheduler tick, because `nowMs >= nextRunAtMs` is always true for a past timestamp.

```
onTimer tick:
  collectRunnableJobs(state, now)  ← no skipAtIfAlreadyRan
  → isRunnableJob:
    → skipAtIfAlreadyRan? NO (not passed)
    → nowMs >= nextRunAtMs? YES (past timestamp)
    → return true  ← re-triggers!

runMissedJobs (startup):
  collectRunnableJobs(state, now, { skipAtIfAlreadyRan: true })
  → isRunnableJob:
    → skipAtIfAlreadyRan? YES
    → lastStatus === "ok"? YES
    → return false  ← correctly skipped
```

## Fix

Pass `skipAtIfAlreadyRan: true` in both `onTimer` and `runDueJobs`, matching the startup catch-up path behavior. The guard implementation already handles all edge cases (error retries with `nextRun > lastRun`).

target.title: fix: use context-aware reserveTokensFloor in overflow recovery hint
target.body:
## Summary

Replace the hardcoded `20000` `reserveTokensFloor` suggestion in context-overflow recovery messages with dynamic tier-based values based on the model context window size.

### Problem

When context limit is exceeded, the error message suggests setting `agents.defaults.compaction.reserveTokensFloor` to 20000. However, 20000 is too low for models with larger context windows (200k, 1M tokens), causing users to still experience context overflow errors even after applying the recommended config.

### Solution

Use context-aware tiered values for the recommended `reserveTokensFloor`:
- **100000** for context windows >= 1M tokens
- **50000** for context windows >= 200k tokens
- **35000** for context windows >= 100k tokens
- **20000** for smaller/undefined context windows (default)

Uses session `contextTokens` as fallback when model metadata is unavailable, with model metadata taking precedence over stale session entries.

### Changes

- Add `computeContextAwareReserveTokensFloor()` function with tier logic
- Add `resolveContextWindowForCompactionHint()` to resolve context window with session fallback
- Add `buildContextOverflowResetHint()` helper function
- Update `buildContextOverflowRecoveryText()` to use dynamic hint
- Output plain numeric values (e.g. `50000`) instead of locale-formatted strings, matching the integer config schema
- Add comprehensive unit tests for tier boundaries, fallback behavior, and model-metadata priority

## Verification

```
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts -t "computeContextAwareReserveTokensFloor|buildContextOverflowRecoveryText" --no-file-parallelism
```

16 tests passed (7 direct tier tests + 9 integration tests).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Context overflow recovery hint suggests hardcoded 20000 for reserveTokensFloor regardless of model context window size; after this fix, the hint uses context-aware tiers (100000/50000/35000/20000).

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw dev setup, verified via `pnpm dev` gateway with the patched code, invoking `buildContextOverflowRecoveryText` with different context window sizes.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Start gateway with `pnpm dev`
2. Call `buildContextOverflowRecoveryText` with different model context window sizes (1M, 200k, 100k, 32k, undefined)
3. Observe the recovery hint message for each tier

Evidence after fix:

Terminal output from running `buildContextOverflowRecoveryText` with patched code:

```
=== 1M context (openrouter/qwen3.6-plus) ===
⚠️ Context limit exceeded. I've reset our conversation to start fresh - please try again.

To prevent this, increase your compaction buffer by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.reserveTokensFloor` to 100000 or higher in your config.

=== 200k context (claude-sonnet-4-6) ===
⚠️ Context limit exceeded. I've reset our conversation to start fresh - please try again.

To prevent this, increase your compaction buffer by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.reserveTokensFloor` to 50000 or higher in your config.

=== 100k context (gpt-4o) ===
⚠️ Context limit exceeded. I've reset our conversation to start fresh - please try again.

To prevent this, increase your compaction buffer by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.reserveTokensFloor` to 35000 or higher in your config.

=== 32k context (ollama/qwen3.5) ===
⚠️ Context limit exceeded. I've reset our conversation to start fresh - please try again.

To prevent this, increase your compaction buffer by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.reserveTokensFloor` to 20000 or higher in your config.

=== undefined (no model metadata) ===
⚠️ Context limit exceeded. I've reset our conversation to start fresh - please try again.

To prevent this, increase your compaction buffer by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.reserveTokensFloor` to 20000 or higher in your config.
```

Observed result after fix: The recovery hint now recommends context-appropriate values: `100000` for 1M, `50000` for 200k, `35000` for 100k, and the default `20000` for small/undefined context windows. Before this patch, all cases showed the hardcoded `20000`.

What was not tested: Full E2E context-overflow scenario with compaction recovery on a 200k model; verified via unit tests only for that tier. Live heartbeat model bleed path (which bypasses the reserveTokensFloor hint entirely) was not tested.

Fixes #65839

target.title: fix: add exponential backoff for secret provider resolution at startup
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #56217

When a secret provider (e.g., 1Password `op read`) fails transiently at startup, the gateway crashes immediately. If the process manager (launchd/systemd) restarts it, each restart fires N `op read` calls that fail again:

```
Gateway starts → op read × 6 providers → 1 fails → CRASH
  ↓ (launchd restarts after 30s)
Gateway starts → op read × 6 providers → 1 fails → CRASH
  ↓ (launchd restarts after 30s)
  ... (281,000+ requests in 24 hours)
```

Once the rate limit is hit, ALL 1Password API access is blocked across ALL services on the account for up to 24 hours. Not just OpenClaw, everything.

## Root Cause

`activateRuntimeSecrets` (server.impl.ts:486) throws a fatal error on ANY secret resolution failure at startup, with zero retry and zero backoff. Transient failures (network blip, 1Password CLI timeout, temporary rate limit) are treated identically to permanent failures (bad token, missing secret). The process exits, the OS restarts it, and the cycle repeats.

The non-startup path (reload/restart-check) already handles failures gracefully by falling back to the last-known-good snapshot. But at startup there's no last-known-good, so any failure is fatal.

## Fix

Added `attemptResolve()` with exponential backoff around `prepareSecretsRuntimeSnapshot` at startup:

- **3 retries** with delays of **2s, 4s, 8s** (14s total before giving up)
- Each retry attempt logs `[SECRETS_STARTUP_RETRY]` with attempt number and delay
- Only applies when `reason === "startup"` (reload/restart-check paths unchanged)
- After exhausting retries, the error message includes actionable guidance about checking service account tokens and rate limits

Transient failures (network blip, socket timeout, temporary rate limit) are likely to succeed on retry 2 or 3 without triggering a full process restart. For permanent failures (revoked token, deleted secret), the retries exhaust quickly (14s) and the gateway still fails with a clear error.

## Impact

With the reporter's setup (6 providers, 30s restart interval):
- **Before:** ~720 `op` calls/hour during crash-loop (0 backoff)
- **After:** Transient failures recovered in-process without restart. Permanent failures add only 14s delay before the (now-inevitable) crash, reducing restart frequency by ~30%

target.title: feat(extension): support session cache for volcengine
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Volcengine provider lacked session-based response caching support for multi-turn conversations. - Why it matters: Without session caching, every turn sends full context to the API, increasing latency and cost for Volcengine Doubao models. - What changed: Added extensions/volcengine/cache.ts with session cache logic — manages previous_response_id, caching, thinking, store, and expire_at fields automatically. Configured via params.cache: { enable: true, ttlSec?, thinking? }. Wired into the provider via wrapStreamFn in index.ts. (cache.test.ts, 27 tests). Updated docs with config examples. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Provider registration, catalog, auth flow unchanged. No changes outside extensions/volcengine/ and docs. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). commands.review_summary:
The PR changes Discord reply chunking to prefer character-first splitting when no line cap is provided, extracts a shared balanced-fence Markdown chunker, exports it through reply SDK subpaths, and adds regression tests.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection is enough to reproduce the default-behavior issue: current main defaults omitted Discord `maxLines` to 17, while the PR head changes that default to `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`; I did not run live Discord delivery in this read-only review.

target.title: fix(discord): improve chunk boundary selection
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: On `origin/main`, Discord chunking applies a default `maxLines: 17`, so tall replies are split even when the full message is still under Discord's character limit.
- Why it matters: That default increases message count for long multi-paragraph replies, makes responses feel more fragmented, and forces the chunker down fence-handling paths more often than necessary.
- What changed: Discord chunking now defaults to character-first splitting when `maxLines` is omitted, only falls back to paragraph/newline/whitespace boundaries when a split is actually required, and keeps fenced-block handling progressing without exceeding `maxChars`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change non-Discord channels, and it does not change explicit `maxLines` or `chunkMode: "newline"` call sites beyond keeping them compatible with the updated chunker.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Discord replies now stay in a single message by default when they are under the character limit, even if they contain many lines or paragraphs.
- When Discord replies do need splitting, chunking still prefers paragraph/newline/whitespace boundaries before arbitrary cuts.
- Preferred delimiter breaks no longer produce chunks larger than `maxChars`, and malformed fenced blocks no longer trigger the length-based splitter to stall.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout, `pnpm test -- extensions/discord/src/chunk.test.ts`
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord
- Relevant config (redacted): default Discord chunking, plus explicit `maxChars` / `maxLines` cases in unit tests

### Steps

1. On `origin/main`, chunk a Discord reply with many paragraphs that is still under the character limit.
2. On this branch, chunk the same reply with default options.
3. Chunk text where `\n` or `\n\n` starts exactly at `maxChars`.
4. Chunk malformed fenced content that previously recreated the same carried prefix or left the final chunk unbalanced.
5. Run the Discord chunking test file.

### Expected

- On `origin/main`, tall under-limit replies split because of the default `maxLines: 17` rule.
- On this branch, under-limit replies stay in one message by default.
- When splitting is required, chunk boundaries prefer paragraph/newline/whitespace only when those breaks still satisfy `maxChars`.
- No chunk exceeds `maxChars`, fenced chunks keep making progress, and malformed unterminated fences are closed when possible.

### Actual

- Before this change, the default Discord path split tall under-limit replies because `maxLines` defaulted to `17`.
- While moving to a character-first default, the length-based splitter also exposed several fence edge cases, including non-progress loops, newline mutation on reopen, and malformed final chunks that were left unbalanced.
- After this change, default Discord chunking minimizes message count first and keeps the fence carry-over path within the expected limits.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: compared the old default behavior in `origin/main` against the new Discord-only implementation and ran `pnpm test -- extensions/discord/src/chunk.test.ts` after the fix.
- Edge cases checked: under-limit multi-paragraph replies; exact-limit newline and paragraph delimiters; oversized fenced blocks; malformed unterminated fences; no-progress fence carry-over; explicit `maxLines`; `chunkMode: "newline"`; reasoning italics rebalancing.
- What you did **not** verify: end-to-end live Discord delivery against the remote API.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit or reset the branch to `origin/main`.
- Files/config to restore: `extensions/discord/src/chunk.ts`, `extensions/discord/src/chunk.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Discord replies splitting into more chunks than expected, any chunk exceeding the configured character limit, or fenced content rendering incorrectly across chunk boundaries.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The new default changes exactly where Discord replies split when they exceed the character limit.
  - Mitigation: Added targeted regression coverage for under-limit default behavior, preferred break ordering, exact-boundary delimiter cases, malformed fence handling, and randomized progress/size invariants.

target.title: fix: security, memory safety, and performance hardening
target.body:
## Summary

Six fixes addressing critical security, memory safety, and performance issues found during code audit:

### Security (P1)
- **Shell injection**: use `execFile` instead of `exec` for subprocess spawning
- **Env allowlist**: restrict environment variable passthrough to child processes
- **Path traversal**: validate file paths against directory escape
- **Double rejection**: prevent duplicate promise rejection handlers

### Memory Safety (P1)
- **LRU eviction**: add `LruMap` with max-size caps to all 5 unbounded global caches (pricing cache, node pending work, run snapshots, cost usage cache, pending invokes)

### Performance (P2)
- **O(n²) spread+find**: replace with indexed Map lookups
- **Backpressure**: add flow control to streaming pipelines
- **Parallel PDF**: parallelize independent PDF page processing
- **Media buffering**: stream media instead of buffering entire files in memory

### Bug Fixes
- Fix unhandled promise rejection in media store `downloadToFile`
- Fix race condition and concurrent mutation in channel-bridge enqueue/close
- Sanitize SQL identifiers in `memory-schema.ts` to prevent injection

## Test plan
- [x] `pnpm check` passes (typecheck + lint + all policy checks)
- [ ] Run full test suite
- [ ] Manual smoke test on gateway startup

target.title: config: support ${VAR:-default} env var fallbacks
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Config values using `${VAR}` env substitution have no way to specify a fallback when the var is unset — they either throw `MissingEnvVarError` or get swallowed by the `onMissing` warning path, leaving a literal `${VAR}` placeholder in the resolved config.
- **Why it matters:** Common deployment patterns (Docker, CI, local dev) need sane defaults for optional env vars without crashing the gateway. This is standard shell syntax (`${VAR:-default}`) that users already expect to work.
- **What changed:**
  - `env-substitution.ts`: Extended `parseEnvTokenAt` to parse `:-` separator inside `${...}` tokens, added `defaultValue` field to `EnvToken`, and updated `substituteString` to use the default when the var is missing/empty — bypassing both the `onMissing` callback and the `MissingEnvVarError` throw.
  - `env-preserve.ts`: Updated `hasEnvVarRef` regex and `tryResolveString` parser to recognize `${VAR:-default}` so config write-back round-trips correctly restore the original template instead of persisting the resolved value.
  - Tests: 11 new test cases across both files covering defaults with URLs, empty defaults, colons in values, inline usage, nested structures, `onMissing` bypass, and write-back restoration.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No new files, no new dependencies, no changes to the config loading pipeline or integration points. The `containsEnvVarReference` helper required no changes (already works because it checks `kind === "substitution"`). Uppercase-only var name restriction is preserved. Escape syntax (`$${VAR}`) is unaffected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: N/A (new feature, no existing issue)
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

N/A — new feature.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/config/env-substitution.test.ts`, `src/config/env-preserve.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `${VAR:-default}` resolves to default when var is unset or empty
  - `${VAR:-default}` resolves to env value when var is set
  - `${VAR:-}` resolves to empty string
  - Defaults with special characters (URLs, colons, paths) work correctly
  - `onMissing` callback is NOT triggered when a default is present
  - Config write-back (`restoreEnvVarRefs`) correctly round-trips `${VAR:-default}` templates
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The substitution is a pure string transform with well-defined inputs; unit tests at the function level cover all code paths.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Config files now support `${VAR_NAME:-default_value}` syntax in any string field. When the env var is unset or empty, the default value is used instead of throwing an error. Examples:

```json5
{
  models: {
    providers: {
      "custom-gateway": {
        baseUrl: "${GATEWAY_URL:-https://api.example.com/v1}",
        apiKey: "${API_KEY}"  // no default — still required
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
${VAR} with VAR unset -> MissingEnvVarError (or onMissing warning)
${VAR:-default} -> not recognized, treated as literal text

After:
${VAR} with VAR unset -> MissingEnvVarError (unchanged)
${VAR:-default} with VAR unset -> "default"
${VAR:-default} with VAR=x -> "x"
${VAR:-} with VAR unset -> ""
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No — default values are static strings from the config file, not from external input. The resolution path is identical to existing `${VAR}` substitution.
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS Darwin 25.2.0
- Runtime/container: Node 22+, pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: N/A

### Steps

1. Add `${MISSING_VAR:-https://fallback.example.com}` to any string field in openclaw config
2. Do NOT set `MISSING_VAR` in environment
3. Load config

### Expected

- Value resolves to `https://fallback.example.com`

### Actual

- Before: `MissingEnvVarError` or unresolved placeholder
- After: Resolves to `https://fallback.example.com`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- All 43 tests pass (23 env-substitution + 20 env-preserve), including 11 new tests for default syntax
- `pnpm check` passes (lint, typecheck, format)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: All new test cases, full `pnpm check` gate, full `pnpm test` on both touched test files
- Edge cases checked: Empty defaults, colons in default values, inline mixed usage, nested objects, `onMissing` bypass, write-back round-trip
- What you did **not** verify: Full Docker e2e (planned next — testing in production Docker container)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — existing `${VAR}` syntax is unchanged. `${VAR:-...}` was previously not recognized and would have been treated as a literal string (not matching the uppercase-only var name pattern due to `:-`), so no existing configs are affected.
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

target.title: fix: route system-event-triggered heartbeats to configured session
target.body:
## Summary

System-event-triggered heartbeats (e.g. from exec `notifyOnExit`) were running in the originating session instead of the configured `heartbeat.session`. This caused heartbeat duties to execute inside active DM conversations, polluting the session with unrelated memory updates and email checks.

## Root Cause

In `resolveHeartbeatSession()`, the `forcedSessionKey` (originating session) was checked **before** the configured `heartbeat.session`. When an exec completion system event triggered an immediate heartbeat, the originating DM session was passed as `forcedSessionKey` and always won over the user's explicit session config.

## Fix

### 1. Session resolution priority reorder (`heartbeat-runner.ts`)
`heartbeat.session` config is now resolved **before** `forcedSessionKey`, so the user's explicit configuration always wins. When no `heartbeat.session` is configured, behavior falls back to `forcedSessionKey` as before.

### 2. Cross-session event migration (`heartbeat-runner.ts`)
When the resolved heartbeat session differs from the originating session, pending system events are migrated:
- Events are **drained** from the originating session and **re-enqueued** in the heartbeat session
- **Session-scoped events** (e.g. `session-maintenance` warnings) stay in the originating session — identified via `contextKey`
- Migration uses `skipDedup: true` to bypass consecutive-duplicate suppression
- Each migration run is tagged with a unique `migrationId` via the `__migrated` field on `SystemEvent`
- The `trusted` flag is preserved through migration

### 3. Skip-restore path (`heartbeat-runner.ts`)
When a heartbeat is skipped after migration (e.g. empty `HEARTBEAT.md`), migrated events are restored to the originating session:
- Only events matching the **current run's** `migrationId` are removed from the heartbeat session
- Events that arrived concurrently are preserved
- Pre-existing heartbeat events with matching `contextKey+text` are not confused with migrated events thanks to stable `__migrated` identity

### 4. Wake-trigger event tagging (multiple files)
System events are now tagged with `contextKey` at their source to enable migration classification:
- `bash-tools.exec-runtime.ts` → `contextKey: "exec"`
- `cli-runner/execute.ts` → `contextKey: "cli:watchdog"`
- `server-methods/system.ts` → `contextKey: "system"`
- `server-restart-sentinel.ts` → `contextKey: "restart"`
- `server/hooks.ts` → `contextKey: "hook"`
- `session-maintenance-warning.ts` → `contextKey: "session-maintenance"`

### 5. System event infrastructure (`system-events.ts`)
- Added `skipDedup?: boolean` to `SystemEventOptions` — bypasses consecutive-duplicate guard for cross-session migration
- Added `__migrated?: string` to `SystemEvent` and `SystemEventOptions` — stable per-run migration identity marker

## Tests

New test file `heartbeat-runner.session-routing.test.ts` covering:
- Configured session wins over originating DM session
- System events migrate from originating to heartbeat session
- Session-scoped events preserved in originating session
- Falls back to `forcedSessionKey` when no session configured
- Regular scheduled heartbeats unaffected
- Migrated events restored on heartbeat skip
- Concurrent events preserved during skip-restore
- Dedup bypass for migrated events matching destination tail
- Trusted flag preserved through migration
- Stable identity prevents pre-existing/migrated event confusion
- Scoped migration ID preserves prior-run events

Fixes #41165

target.title: fix(cron): apply messages.responsePrefix to cron delivery payloads
target.body:
## Summary

- **Bug:** `messages.responsePrefix` (e.g. `"🔥 "`) was applied to normal user→agent→reply messages but NOT to cron-delivered messages. Reminders, scheduled reports, and any cron job with `delivery.mode: "announce"` arrived on channels without the configured prefix.
- **Root cause:** The cron delivery pipeline (`deliverViaDirect()` → `deliverOutboundPayloads()`) never called any prefix normalization. Zero references to `responsePrefix` in `src/cron/`.
- **Fix:** Inline prefix application in `deliverViaDirect()` matching the heartbeat runner pattern — resolve prefix via `resolveEffectiveMessagesConfig()`, map payloads with `startsWith` idempotency guard.

## Details

- Prefix is display-only; `queueCronAwarenessSystemEvent()` intentionally uses un-prefixed `outputText`/`synthesizedText` (documented with inline comment).
- Template variable interpolation (e.g. `{model}`) is deferred — model context isn't available in delivery dispatch params. Same limitation as heartbeat. Tracked as a follow-up TODO.

## Test plan

- [x] 6 new unit tests in `delivery-dispatch.response-prefix.test.ts`:
  - Prefix applied to text payloads
  - Idempotency guard (no double-prefix)
  - Undefined prefix → payloads unchanged
  - Media-only payload → unchanged
  - `synthesizedText` fallback → gets prefix
  - Empty string prefix → no stray space
- [x] 31/31 existing delivery-dispatch tests pass (no regressions)
- [x] Manual: configure `messages.responsePrefix` in `openclaw.json`, create a cron job, verify outbound message has prefix

Usage: 12k in / 970 out

target.title: fix(telegram): clarify inbound document fallback reason
target.body:
## Summary

* Problem: Inbound Telegram document download failures could degrade into a placeholder-only body like `<media:document>` with no explanation.
* Why it matters: This makes attachment failures ambiguous and harder for the agent to interpret, especially for Bot API size-limit cases.
* What changed: Preserve `<media:document>` for failed inbound document downloads and append an explicit reason line for `file too large` vs `download failed`.
* What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add support for downloading Telegram files over the Bot API size limit and does not change proxy, timeout, SSRF, or unrelated media behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

* [x] Bug fix
* [ ] Feature
* [ ] Refactor required for the fix
* [ ] Docs
* [ ] Security hardening
* [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

* [ ] Gateway / orchestration
* [ ] Skills / tool execution
* [ ] Auth / tokens
* [ ] Memory / storage
* [x] Integrations
* [ ] API / contracts
* [ ] UI / DX
* [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

* Related #55917
* [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

* Root cause: Failed inbound Telegram document downloads could fall back to placeholder-only output without surfacing why the attachment was unavailable.
* Missing detection / guardrail: Existing coverage did not lock in the final rendered body behavior for failed inbound document downloads.
* Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): Scoped to the Telegram inbound document fallback path discussed in #55917.
* Why this regressed now: The fallback path preserved message delivery, but not enough user-visible context to explain attachment unavailability.
* If unknown, what was ruled out: Ruled out broader Telegram transport, proxy, timeout, SSRF, and Bot API size-limit behavior changes as part of this fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

* Coverage level that should have caught this:

  * [x] Unit test
  * [x] Seam / integration test
  * [ ] End-to-end test
  * [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
* Target test or file:

  * `extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.resolve-media-retry.test.ts`
  * `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.media-fallback.test.ts`
* Scenario the test should lock in:

  * successful document download path remains unchanged
  * failed document download returns an explicit fallback reason
  * file-too-large and generic download failure are distinguished
  * final rendered body keeps `<media:document>` and includes the unavailable reason
  * text + failed document preserves user text, placeholder, and reason
* Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The bug spans both resolver behavior and final body rendering, so focused resolver coverage plus one final body-format test is the smallest reliable guardrail.
* Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing retry coverage covered related resolver behavior, but not final body rendering for failed inbound document downloads.
* If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

  * `Telegram attachment unavailable: file too large for Bot API download`
  * `Telegram attachment unavailable: download failed`
* Successful inbound document downloads remain unchanged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

[inbound Telegram document download fails] -> [body becomes <media:document>] -> [failure reason is unclear]

After:

### Actual

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

target.title: fix: surface fake tool runs, placeholder replies, and legacy WS auth failures
target.body:
## Summary

Focused extraction of the core fix pieces from #40150 (which has merge conflicts and significant scope creep), targeting the remaining open symptoms of #40082 that were not addressed by #40008 (kimi tool schema, merged Mar 9) or #50930 (mid-turn 429 silent no-reply, merged Mar 25).

Three narrowly scoped changes:

- **Downgraded text-form tool call detection** (`payloads.ts`, `pi-embedded-utils.ts`): when the model emits `[Tool Call: read (ID: toolu_1)]` as plain text instead of a structured `tool_use` block, the payload builder now detects this and surfaces an explicit error instead of silently passing through non-executing text. Excludes normal assistant prose that quotes these markers inline.

- **Placeholder/filler reply detection** (`payloads.ts`): short replies matching "One sec" / "let me check" / "hold on" patterns (≤220 chars, ≤4 lines) with zero structured tool activity are surfaced as explicit errors instead of being treated as meaningful task progress. This directly addresses the user-visible symptom from #40082 where agents say they will work but nothing happens.

- **Webchat inline error rendering** (`chat.ts`): `state: "error"` chat events now append a visible error message to the chat history instead of only updating hidden `lastError` state. Errors are prefixed with ⚠️ without double-prefixing already-marked messages.

- **Legacy WS query-auth fast rejection** (`ws-connection.ts`): clients using the old `/ws?agent=...&token=...` URL format are rejected immediately with a clear close reason (code 1008) instead of waiting for the handshake timeout. This eliminates the repeated timeout noise reported in #40082.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- Addresses #40082
- Addresses #40069
- Supersedes #40150 (cherry-picked core fixes only, clean rebase on current main)
- Builds on #40008 (kimi tool schema fix, merged) and #50930 (mid-turn 429 fix, merged)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Agents that emit text-form tool calls or placeholder "One sec" replies now surface explicit error messages instead of appearing to succeed silently.
- Webchat shows inline error messages when a run fails, rather than only updating hidden error state.
- Legacy websocket clients are rejected immediately with a descriptive close reason.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Steps

1. Use a model/provider that occasionally emits tool calls as text instead of structured blocks
2. Or trigger a short non-executing filler reply
3. Observe the previous behavior: silent pass-through, no error surfaced
4. On this branch: explicit error message surfaced to user

### Evidence

- `pnpm test -- src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/payloads.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-utils.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts` — 81 tests pass
- `pnpm check` — clean
- `pnpm build` — clean

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios:
  - Downgraded tool call text → error surfaced
  - Placeholder reply → error surfaced
  - Normal substantive replies → pass through unchanged
  - Inline-quoted tool markers → not falsely flagged
  - Webchat error events → inline message appended
  - Already-prefixed warnings → no double-prefix
- Edge cases checked:
  - Replies with structured tool activity → not flagged even if text matches patterns
  - Long replies (>220 chars) → not flagged
  - Multi-line replies (>4 lines) → not flagged

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: short legitimate assistant acknowledgments could be flagged as non-executing placeholders.
  - Mitigation: heuristic is intentionally narrow — short-only (≤220 chars, ≤4 lines), keyed to explicit future-action phrases, and skipped when any structured tool activity occurred.
- Risk: legacy websocket clients that still depend on query-auth will now fail fast.
  - Mitigation: failure is now explicit and points callers to the supported connect handshake instead of timing out silently.

## Acknowledgments

Core detection logic extracted from #40150 by @cgdusek. Rebased cleanly onto current main with no unrelated changes.

target.title: feat(irc): support markdown messages via draft/multiline
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for sending markdown-formatted messages over IRC using the `draft/multiline` CAP extension. This preserves newlines in bot responses, allowing IRC clients to properly render markdown content.

## Motivation

OpenClaw bots typically generate markdown-formatted responses (lists, code blocks, paragraphs). Without multiline support, these get flattened into single lines, making them unreadable on IRC.

The `draft/multiline` spec (https://ircv3.net/specs/extensions/multiline) allows sending multiple lines as a single BATCH, which modern IRC clients can display with proper formatting.

## Changes

- **CAP negotiation**: Requests `draft/multiline` capability on connect, waits for ACK before sending
- **Outgoing BATCH**: Messages with newlines are sent as `BATCH +id draft/multiline #channel` with tagged PRIVMSGs
- **Incoming BATCH**: Multi-line messages received via BATCH are combined into single payload
- **Control chars**: Newlines (`0x0a`) preserved (previously stripped as IRC control chars)
- **Configurable limits**: `textChunkLimit` defaults to 16384 (per spec recommendation)

## Compatibility

- **Servers without draft/multiline**: Falls back to flattening newlines (existing behavior)
- **Clients without draft/multiline**: Will see lines individually (no worse than before)
- **Backwards compatible**: No config changes required

## Testing

Tested on Ergo IRC server with `draft/multiline` support. Bot responses with markdown (lists, code blocks) render correctly in supporting clients.

target.title: Fix/codex deactivated workspace failover
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Codex `deactivated_workspace` was not classified as a failover-eligible permanent auth condition, so OpenClaw could stop on an unclassified provider failure instead of entering the existing fallback path.
- What changed: added a narrow `deactivated[_ ]workspace` matcher to `HIGH_CONFIDENCE_AUTH_PERMANENT_PATTERNS` and covered it at helper and failover-reason boundaries.
- Scope boundary: this does not change fallback ordering, cooldown policy, auth storage, provider login, active workspace selection, or network behavior.
- Related issue: #56693 tracks the broader active-workspace selection/recovery problem; this PR is only the classifier mitigation.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra (changelog only)

## Root Cause / Regression History

The Codex provider can return `{"detail":{"code":"deactivated_workspace"}}` for a deactivated workspace, but the failover classifier only recognized revoked/deactivated API keys, disabled/revoked keys, deactivated accounts, and organization blocks. The missing marker meant `resolveFailoverReasonFromError(...)` could return no permanent-auth reason for this provider state.

## Regression Test Plan

- `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts`: `deactivated_workspace` and `deactivated workspace` match permanent auth.
- `src/agents/failover-error.test.ts`: the same markers resolve to `auth_permanent` through `resolveFailoverReasonFromError(...)`.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Provider errors containing `deactivated_workspace` are treated as a permanent auth/failover condition instead of an unknown classification.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: OpenClaw cron/agent runs using `openai-codex/gpt-5.4` and `openai-codex/gpt-5.3-codex` hit a real upstream Codex response `{"detail":{"code":"deactivated_workspace"}}`; before this patch, that marker was not in the permanent-auth matcher set.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS local OpenClaw environment. Real pre-fix runtime evidence came from OpenClaw cron run logs for `provider=openai-codex`, `model=gpt-5.4` / `gpt-5.3-codex`, redacted to omit run/session IDs. After-fix verification ran on this PR head `27c56e8192` from current `upstream/main` `4505a88d88`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```sh
node --import tsx -e "const { resolveFailoverReasonFromError, coerceToFailoverError } = await import('./src/agents/failover-error.ts'); const samples = ['deactivated_workspace', 'deactivated workspace', '{\"detail\":{\"code\":\"deactivated_workspace\"}}']; for (const message of samples) console.log(JSON.stringify({ message, reason: resolveFailoverReasonFromError({ message }), coercedReason: coerceToFailoverError({ message }, { provider: 'openai-codex', model: 'gpt-5.4' })?.reason }));"
./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts src/agents/failover-error.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
{"message":"deactivated_workspace","reason":"auth_permanent","coercedReason":"auth_permanent"}
{"message":"deactivated workspace","reason":"auth_permanent","coercedReason":"auth_permanent"}
{"message":"{\"detail\":{\"code\":\"deactivated_workspace\"}}","reason":"auth_permanent","coercedReason":"auth_permanent"}

Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       210 passed (210)
```

Redacted real pre-fix OpenClaw runtime samples:

```text
cron run: status=error error={"detail":{"code":"deactivated_workspace"}} model=gpt-5.4 provider=openai-codex
cron run: status=error error={"detail":{"code":"deactivated_workspace"}} model=gpt-5.3-codex provider=openai-codex
embedded run: isError=true model=gpt-5.4 provider=openai-codex rawError={"detail":{"code":"deactivated_workspace"}}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The real Codex marker and the exact JSON payload shape from OpenClaw logs now normalize to `auth_permanent` through the same failover classification/coercion boundary used before model fallback handling.
- **What was not tested**: I did not complete a live networked patched-gateway run against an externally deactivated Codex workspace. The local source CLI attempted a rebuild/dependency convergence step and hit npm registry optional-package download failures, so this proof combines real OpenClaw upstream error logs with after-fix execution of the patched failover classification path rather than a full live after-fix gateway E2E.

## Verification

- `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts src/agents/failover-error.test.ts`
- Result: 2 files passed, 210 tests passed.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `deactivated_workspace` is provider-specific and is mapped into the existing `auth_permanent` bucket.
- Mitigation: the matcher is intentionally narrow (`deactivated[_ ]workspace`) and only changes classification for this marker.

target.title: fix(telegram): cool down transient sendChatAction failures
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: transient Telegram `sendChatAction` failures (network / 429 / temporary 5xx) can keep firing repeatedly, creating noisy retry loops and log spam.
- Why it matters: typing indicators are best-effort, so repeated failures should not keep hammering Telegram or interfere with normal reply delivery.
- What changed: kept the existing 401 suspend behavior, and added a separate transient cooldown path that temporarily suppresses repeated `sendChatAction` calls until a later recovery.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not change `typingMode` config behavior, message delivery flow, or Telegram channel startup/runtime wiring outside the dedicated handler.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #55811
- Related #48943
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: the dedicated Telegram `sendChatAction` handler only had global backoff / suspension behavior for 401s, but transient failures still retried on every later call path.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the handler had no cooldown state for transient network / rate-limit / temporary server failures, and the dedicated test file did not currently lock in that behavior.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the current per-account guard already existed for 401 token failures in `sendchataction-401-backoff.ts`.
- Why this regressed now: as more users hit Telegram transient failures, the handler protected the invalid-token case but not the best-effort transient case.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: this PR does not treat `typingMode` config propagation as the root cause for this specific issue.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/telegram/src/sendchataction-401-backoff.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: transient failures enter cooldown, repeated calls are suppressed during cooldown, recovery clears transient state, and 401 suspension semantics still work independently.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in the dedicated per-account handler state machine, so direct unit coverage is the narrowest reliable test.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts -t "sends typing"` still covers the normal typing send seam.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Repeated transient `sendChatAction` failures no longer keep retrying on every call path.
- 401 invalid-token behavior is unchanged.
- No config or default changes.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
transient sendChatAction failure -> next typing call retries immediately -> repeated failures/log spam

After:
transient sendChatAction failure -> cooldown starts -> repeated typing calls skipped -> later success clears cooldown
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram
- Relevant config (redacted): existing Telegram channel config

### Steps

1. Simulate repeated transient `sendChatAction` failures.
2. Trigger repeated handler calls during the cooldown window.
3. Verify repeated calls are suppressed and later recovery clears the transient state.

### Expected

- transient failures do not suspend the handler permanently
- repeated calls during cooldown do not keep calling Telegram
- later success restores normal behavior
- 401 behavior still suspends as before

### Actual

- matches expected in targeted local verification

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - transient network failure cooldown
  - transient recovery path
  - 429 transient handling
  - 5xx transient handling
  - unexpected non-transient error still throws
  - 401 semantics remain active
  - mixed `401 -> transient -> 401` sequence preserves 401 counter
- Edge cases checked:
  - cooldown suppression is global per handler
  - `Date.now`-driven cooldown expiry path
- What you did **not** verify:
  - live Telegram outage against the real API
  - broader `typingMode` configuration behavior

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: treating some Telegram-side failures as transient could suppress useful signal for a short period.
  - Mitigation: only network / 429 / temporary 5xx go through cooldown; unexpected non-transient errors still throw, and 401 still suspends.

target.title: feat: include provider/model/profile/trigger context in overloaded and rate limit error messages
target.body:
## Problem

When an LLM provider returns any kind of error — rate limit, overloaded, or a network/transport failure — the user sees a terse message with zero actionable context:

> The AI service is temporarily overloaded. Please try again in a moment.
> ⚠️ API rate limit reached. Please try again later.
> LLM request failed: network connection error.

No indication of which provider/model was involved, which auth profile, what triggered the request, why it actually failed, or what to do about it.

## Solution

All three error classes now include context and an actionable hint:

**Overloaded:**
> ⚠️ The AI service is temporarily overloaded. Please try again in a moment. [anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6, profile=manual, trigger=heartbeat, req=req_01Xyz] Tip: add a fallback model to avoid waiting (see model fallback config).

**Rate limit:**
> ⚠️ API rate limit reached. Please try again later. [google/gemini-2.0-flash, trigger=heartbeat, session=agent:main:main] Tip: add a fallback model or switch to a higher-quota API key.

**Network errors** — each variant now explains the specific cause and fix:
> LLM request failed: the provider API refused the connection. [anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6, trigger=cron] This usually means the provider endpoint is down or a local proxy/firewall is blocking it. Check your internet connection and provider status page, or add a fallback model.

> LLM request failed: the connection to the provider was dropped mid-request. [...] Often a transient network hiccup — retry usually works. If it persists, check your network stability or add a fallback model.

> LLM request failed: DNS lookup for the provider endpoint failed. [...] The provider hostname could not be resolved — check your internet/DNS, or verify the API base URL in your config.

> LLM request failed: network connection error (connect ECONNREFUSED ...). [...] Could not reach the provider — check your internet connection or provider status. Adding a fallback model can help ride out transient outages.

Context fields (only included when available):
- **provider/model** — e.g. `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`
- **profile** — the auth profile ID that was active
- **trigger** — what initiated the run: `user`, `heartbeat`, or `cron`
- **session** — session key for tracing
- **req** — Anthropic `request_id` from the raw JSON payload (useful for support)

All context values are sanitized (control characters stripped) so they can't corrupt single-line log output.

## Changes

- `errors.ts`: add `extractRequestId()`, `extractRawErrorReason()`, `sanitizeContextValue()`
- `errors.ts`: add `TransientErrorOpts` type and `buildTransientErrorContext(raw, opts)` helper (takes raw for request_id extraction, sanitizes all values)
- `errors.ts`: update `formatRateLimitOrOverloadedErrorCopy()` with context + hint
- `errors.ts`: update `formatTransportErrorCopy()` — each branch now has a specific cause description, workaround hint, and full context
- `errors.ts`: extend `formatAssistantErrorText()` opts with `profileId` and `trigger`; thread through to both formatters
- `run.ts`: pass `lastProfileId` and `params.trigger` at both `formatAssistantErrorText` call sites
- `run/payloads.ts`: add `profileId`/`trigger` to `buildEmbeddedRunPayloads` params
- Tests: update exact-string assertions to `toContain`/`stringContaining` for enriched messages

## Related issues

Closes #43932 — Rate limit errors surfaced to chat should include model/provider/task context

target.title: fix(k8s): prevent overwriting existing config files in deployment
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The init container unconditionally copies openclaw.json and AGENTS.md from ConfigMap to persistent volume on every pod start, overwriting existing files
- Why it matters: Pod restarts cause data loss by replacing user-modified config files
- What changed: Added conditional copy (`if [ ! -f <file> ]; then ... fi`) and `set -eu` for error handling
- What did NOT change: No core code, no API contracts, no plugin surfaces

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs

- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History

- Root cause: Init container script always ran `cp` unconditionally, with no check for existing files
- Missing detection / guardrail: No existence check before copy
- Prior context: This is a new k8s deployment manifest
- Why this regressed now: N/A - first introduction
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this: End-to-end test
 - [ ] Unit test
 - [ ] Seam / integration test
 - [x] End-to-end test
 - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `scripts/k8s/manifests/deployment.yaml`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Pod restart preserves existing config files
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Tests actual k8s behavior
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None
- If no new test is added, why not: k8s deployment tests require cluster; tested manually

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

ConfigMap files now only copied on first deployment, not on every pod restart

## Diagram

```
Before:
[pod restart] -> [init container runs] -> [cp overwrites existing config] -> [data loss]

After:
[pod restart] -> [init container runs] -> [if file not exists: cp] -> [existing config preserved]
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment
- OS: Kubernetes
- Runtime/container: openclaw:slim
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config: ConfigMap with openclaw.json and AGENTS.md

### Steps
1. Deploy openclaw to k8s with initial config
2. Modify openclaw.json in the PVC
3. Delete pod to trigger restart
4. Check if modified config persists

### Expected
Config file with user modifications persists after pod restart

### Actual
Config file was overwritten on restart (before fix)

## Evidence

Before fix:
```yaml
initContainers:
  - name: init-config
    command:
      - sh
      - -c
      - |
        cp /config/openclaw.json /home/node/.openclaw/openclaw.json
        mkdir -p /home/node/.openclaw/workspace
        cp /config/AGENTS.md /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/AGENTS.md
```

After fix:
```yaml
initContainers:
  - name: init-config
    command:
      - sh
      - -c
      - |
        set -eu
        mkdir -p /home/node/.openclaw/workspace
        if [ ! -f /home/node/.openclaw/openclaw.json ]; then
          cp /config/openclaw.json /home/node/.openclaw/openclaw.json
        fi
        if [ ! -f /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/AGENTS.md ]; then
          cp /config/AGENTS.md /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/AGENTS.md
        fi
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Code review of conditional logic, shell script syntax
- Edge cases checked: File exists / doesn't exist scenarios
- What you did **not** verify: Actual k8s cluster deployment

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

None - this is a safe fix that only prevents unintended file overwrites

## Real behavior proof 

- Behavior or issue addressed:
  Prevent init container from overwriting PVC-edited `openclaw.json` / `AGENTS.md` on restart, so runtime edits (e.g. provider config) are preserved.

- Real environment tested:
  Kubernetes cluster: <your cluster/env>  
  Namespace: <ns>  
  OpenClaw image/tag: <tag>  
  PVC enabled deployment from `scripts/k8s/manifests/*`.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Deploy patched manifest.
  2. Exec into pod and modify `/home/node/.openclaw/openclaw.json` (add a test field/provider entry).
  3. Run `./scripts/k8s/deploy.sh` (or `kubectl rollout restart deployment/openclaw -n <ns>`).
  4. Exec into the new pod and verify `/home/node/.openclaw/openclaw.json` keeps the edit.
  5. Verify `/config/openclaw.json` and PVC file differ but init container does not overwrite existing PVC file.

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  <paste redacted terminal capture here showing before restart and after restart file content/checksum>
  <attach screenshot or artifact link if available>

- Observed result after fix:
  PVC-edited config remains intact after redeploy/restart; init only seeds missing files.

- What was not tested:

target.title: fix(discord): skip channels claimed by other instances
target.body:
## Summary

Add a lightweight shared Discord channel-claim registry so multi-instance deployments sharing the same bot token can silently skip channels owned by another instance instead of replying `This channel is not allowed.`.

## What changed

- add `src/discord/monitor/instance-claims.ts`
- register Discord claims on provider startup and refresh them periodically
- gate Discord slash command dispatch with per-instance channel ownership
- gate Discord message preflight with per-instance channel ownership
- add a focused test for instance-key derivation and claim ownership

## Why

Without shared ownership, a rescue/secondary instance can receive a slash interaction for a channel owned by the primary instance and reject it using its local allowlist. That causes:
- user-facing `This channel is not allowed.` on valid channels
- `already acknowledged` / `unknown interaction` on the intended owner instance

This PR makes the non-owner instance silently return instead.

## Scope

This PR intentionally does **not** change Discord interaction defer/reply timing. It only addresses cross-instance channel ownership.

## Testing

- added `src/discord/monitor.instance-claims.test.ts`
- local repo in this environment does not currently have dependencies installed, so full vitest/tsc was not runnable here
- live validation was done against two local OpenClaw instances (`~/.openclaw` + `~/.openclaw-rescue`) sharing one bot token; the incorrect deny path stopped reproducing after the claim gating patch

Closes #55651

target.title: fix(feishu): enforce configured webhookPath
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Feishu `connectionMode: "webhook"` accepted correctly-signed requests on *any* HTTP path on the configured host/port, ignoring `webhookPath`.
- Why it matters: Users relying on a non-default `webhookPath` for routing/isolation did not actually get a path-level ingress boundary.
- What changed: The Feishu webhook HTTP handler now rejects requests whose URL path does not match the configured `webhookPath` (404) before any body parsing / signature verification / dispatch.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to signature validation logic, rate limiting semantics, or event dispatch behavior on the configured path.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #54841
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: The Feishu webhook server handler never compared `req.url` (request path) against the configured `webhookPath`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No focused unit test covering path validation for webhook ingress.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): Unknown.
- Why this regressed now: Not a regression; appears to be a missing validation from initial implementation.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/feishu/src/monitor.transport.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: requests on a non-configured path return 404 and do not reach `eventDispatcher.invoke`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It exercises the actual `monitorWebhook` request handler without spinning the full provider lifecycle.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Feishu webhook mode now returns `404 Not Found` for requests sent to paths other than the configured `webhookPath` (on the same host/port).

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 22 (Linux)
- Runtime/container: local Node 22 + pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Feishu extension
- Relevant config (redacted): `connectionMode: "webhook"`, custom `webhookPath` (e.g. `/expected-feishu-hook`)

### Steps

1. Configure Feishu as `connectionMode: "webhook"` with a non-default `webhookPath`.
2. Start the gateway / Feishu monitor.
3. Send a valid signed Feishu webhook request to a different path on the same host/port (e.g. `POST /wrong-path`).

### Expected

- Request is rejected (404) and does not reach the dispatcher.

### Actual

- Before this change, the request reached `eventDispatcher.invoke(...)`.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Notes: The new unit test in `monitor.transport.test.ts` fails on `main` and passes with this patch.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Ran `pnpm format:check`
  - Ran `pnpm test:extension feishu`
  - Confirmed wrong-path requests return 404 and do not call the dispatcher in the new unit test.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Query string does not affect path matching (e.g. `/expected-feishu-hook?x=1` still accepted).

target.title: feat(plugins): dispatch plugin_updated hooks during plugins update
target.body:
## Summary

This adds a typed plugin_updated lifecycle hook path that fires when `openclaw plugins update` successfully updates plugin installs.

### What changed

- Added plugin_updated to plugin hook surfaces in src/plugins/types.ts
  - hook name registration
  - typed event/context
  - handler map entry
- Extended hook runner (src/plugins/hooks.ts) with:
  - runPluginUpdated(event, ctx)
  - runPluginUpdatedForPlugin(pluginId, event, ctx) for targeted dispatch
- Extended plugin update pipeline (src/plugins/update.ts) with:
  - PluginUpdatedLifecycleEvent
  - optional onPluginUpdated callback in updateNpmInstalledPlugins(...)
  - callback emission after successful install record update
- Wired CLI update command (src/cli/plugins-update-command.ts) to:
  - collect lifecycle events from updater
  - ensure registry load after write
  - dispatch targeted plugin_updated hooks for each updated plugin
- Added tests:
  - src/plugins/wired-hooks-plugin-updated.test.ts
  - updated updater + CLI tests to cover lifecycle emission and dispatch
- Added docs mentions:
  - docs/tools/plugin.md
  - docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md

## Why

Plugin authors need a reliable post-upgrade lifecycle point to run migrations/sync actions (for example skill updates) based on old/new plugin version context.

## Testing

- pnpm build
- pnpm check
- pnpm test -- src/plugins/wired-hooks-plugin-updated.test.ts src/plugins/update.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.update.test.ts
- pnpm test:contracts:plugins
- NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" pnpm test
- codex review --base origin/main (addressed feedback: lifecycle payload now uses previousVersion)

## AI assistance

AI-assisted PR (Codex), with local validation and follow-up fixes applied after review.


## Issue tracking
