target.title: fix: dedupe OpenAI strict schema downgrade diagnostics
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenAI/Azure OpenAI strict tool schema downgrade diagnostics repeated on nearly every request when the visible tool inventory was not strict-compatible.
- Why it matters: repeated debug lines hid new provider diagnostics and made the same known downgrade look like fresh failures.
- What changed: the downgrade diagnostic is now keyed by provider/model/transport/tool-violation signature and logged once per unique signature per process.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not alter strict-mode selection or repair incompatible schemas; incompatible inventories still send `strict=false`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #82930
- Related #80926
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: repeated `OpenAI responses tool schema strict mode downgraded to strict=false` diagnostics for the same Azure OpenAI Responses provider/model/tool-violation signature.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw worktree, Node v24.15.0, focused OpenAI transport test file exercising the request-build diagnostic seam with a mocked OpenAI transport logger.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied terminal capture:

```text
RUN  v4.1.6

✓ |agents-core| ../../src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts > openai transport stream > deduplicates repeated OpenAI strict schema downgrade diagnostics 256ms
✓ |agents-support| ../../src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts > openai transport stream > deduplicates repeated OpenAI strict schema downgrade diagnostics 180ms

Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  338 passed (338)
Duration  16.04s
```

- Observed result after fix: the new regression imports the transport module in isolation with debug logging enabled, calls `buildOpenAIResponsesParams` twice for the same strict-incompatible provider/model/tool signature, verifies both payloads keep `strict=false`, and verifies the mocked logger receives exactly one downgrade diagnostic.
- What was not tested: no live Azure OpenAI Responses rerun was performed because the evidence came from a private affected gateway log/session; private deployment names, session identifiers, local paths, and job names were redacted and not reused publicly.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): redacted local log evidence showed 2,068 lines matching `strict mode downgraded`; lines 227, 238, 247, 259, and 268 repeated `OpenAI responses tool schema strict mode downgraded to strict=false for azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.3-codex because 13 tool schema(s) are not strict-compatible` within seconds.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `resolveOpenAIStrictToolFlagWithDiagnostics` logged every debug-enabled strict downgrade without remembering that the same provider/model/transport/tool-violation set had already been reported.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression coverage for repeated downgrade diagnostics.
- Contributing context (if known): OpenAI strict schema compatibility is inventory-wide, so one incompatible visible tool inventory can downgrade every request until schemas are fixed or the tool surface changes.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: building the same native OpenAI Responses request twice with the same strict-incompatible tool signature keeps `strict=false` while emitting one downgrade diagnostic.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the failure is in request-build diagnostic emission, before provider generation; an isolated module import with a mocked logger exercises the production `buildOpenAIResponsesParams` path without adding test-only source exports or requiring live provider credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

OpenAI strict schema downgrade debug logs are less noisy for repeated identical tool-schema incompatibilities. Provider payload strictness behavior is unchanged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[same incompatible tool inventory] -> [strict=false] -> [debug line on every request]

After:
[same incompatible tool inventory] -> [strict=false] -> [debug line once per unique signature]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: local source checkout; original affected OS not enough info from redacted evidence
- Runtime/container: Node v24.15.0
- Model/provider: `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.3-codex` in before evidence; local regression uses native OpenAI Responses model metadata
- Integration/channel (if any): OpenAI transport
- Relevant config (redacted): native Azure OpenAI Responses route with strict tool shaping enabled and 13 strict-incompatible schemas in the visible tool inventory

### Steps

1. Enable debug logging for OpenAI transport/tool schema diagnostics.
2. Send repeated requests using the same native OpenAI/Azure OpenAI Responses model and same strict-incompatible tool inventory.
3. Observe repeated downgrade diagnostics before the patch; after the patch, identical signatures are suppressed after the first log.

### Expected

- Identical strict downgrade diagnostics are emitted once per unique provider/model/transport/tool-violation signature.

### Actual

- Before the patch, the same downgrade diagnostic repeated on nearly every request.
- After the patch, the focused regression test proves identical signatures are deduplicated.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: focused OpenAI transport test file passes after adding the behavior-level dedupe regression.
- Edge cases checked: the repeated same-signature request path keeps `strict=false` for both payload builds while suppressing only the duplicate debug diagnostic.
- What you did **not** verify: live Azure OpenAI Responses rerun with the private affected session/provider setup.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a later identical downgrade diagnostic is suppressed even if an operator wanted repeated per-request reminders.
  - Mitigation: changed provider/model/transport/tool-violation signatures still log, and strict false behavior is unchanged.

target.title: feat(hooks): add agent:turn:end internal hook event
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: ACP turn completion updates latency/error stats, but there is still no internal hook that fires once a turn fully completes, so post-turn automation cannot reliably observe a single end-of-turn seam.
- Why it matters: Existing signals are per-message, sub-turn, or session-level, which makes analytics and post-turn workflows awkward and error-prone.
- What changed: Added a narrow `agent:turn:end` internal hook emitted from `recordTurnCompletion()` on both success and error paths, with targeted tests for the event type guard and manager-level emission.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add `agent:turn:start`, new config, or broader lifecycle refactors.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #37833
- Related #60734
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Internal hook consumers can now subscribe to `agent:turn:end` and receive a once-per-turn completion signal.
- The hook carries `sessionKey`, `success`, `durationMs`, and optional `errorCode` on failures.
- No user-facing messaging or config behavior changes.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[ACP turn completes] -> [latency/error stats only]

After:
[ACP turn completes] -> [latency/error stats] -> [agent:turn:end internal hook] -> [state transition continues]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default local test config

### Steps

1. Register an internal hook listener for `agent:turn:end`.
2. Trigger a successful ACP turn and a failing ACP turn.
3. Verify the hook fires once per completed turn with the expected context and does not break the turn result.

### Expected

- Success path emits `success: true` with `durationMs`.
- Failure path emits `success: false` and `errorCode`.
- Hook listener failures do not break the turn lifecycle.

### Actual

- Added manager-level tests covering successful and failed turns.
- Added/updated internal-hook type guard coverage.
- Exact requested focused tests passed: `pnpm test src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts`.
- Exact requested formatting gate passed: `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/acp/control-plane/manager.core.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts CHANGELOG.md docs/automation/hooks.md`.
- Changed-file quality gate passed: `pnpm check:changed`.
- GitHub checks are green for commit `72ca7a44f2548bb172d8e1b5f004b7b8df50e8c3`, including `Real behavior proof`.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `agent:turn:end` now fires after ACP turn completion on both success and failure, and the failure payload preserves `errorCode` (`ACP_TURN_FAILED`).
- **Real environment tested**: macOS local OpenClaw source checkout at `/Users/mantis/Projects/openclaw-60734-agent-turn-end-hook`, branch `feat/37833-agent-turn-end-hook`, commit `72ca7a44f2548bb172d8e1b5f004b7b8df50e8c3`, using the production `AcpSessionManager` and internal-hook registry against an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran a live `node --import tsx --input-type=module` script that registered an ACP runtime backend, registered an `agent:turn:end` listener, initialized one success ACP session and one failure ACP session through `AcpSessionManager.initializeSession()`, then executed both turns through `AcpSessionManager.runTurn()`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from that command:

  ```text
  failed turn error code: ACP_TURN_FAILED
  [
    {
      "sessionKey": "agent:codex:acp:cpr-proof-success",
      "success": true,
      "durationMs": 2
    },
    {
      "sessionKey": "agent:codex:acp:cpr-proof-failure",
      "success": false,
      "durationMs": 0,
      "errorCode": "ACP_TURN_FAILED"
    }
  ]
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: The hook listener received exactly one completion payload for the successful turn and exactly one completion payload for the failed turn; the failed turn also propagated `ACP_TURN_FAILED` and the hook payload included `errorCode: "ACP_TURN_FAILED"`.
- **What was not tested**: This proof did not exercise a networked external `acpx` adapter process; it verified the OpenClaw manager and internal-hook behavior in a local source checkout with an isolated state directory.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `agent:turn:end` type guard recognizes the new event shape, including optional `errorCode`
  - ACP manager emits the hook after successful turns
  - ACP manager emits the hook after failed turns
  - failure-path emission preserves `errorCode: "ACP_TURN_FAILED"`
  - hook dispatch uses `fireAndForgetBoundedHook(() => triggerInternalHook(...))`
  - docs and changelog include the new hook surface
- Commands run locally:
  - `pnpm test src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts`
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/acp/control-plane/manager.core.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts CHANGELOG.md docs/automation/hooks.md`
  - `pnpm check:changed`
- Real behavior proof:
  - Ran a local source-checkout `AcpSessionManager` proof with an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`; observed one success hook payload and one failed hook payload with `errorCode: "ACP_TURN_FAILED"`.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - a networked external `acpx` adapter process; this PR verifies the OpenClaw ACP manager and internal-hook seam.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Hook timing may be reviewed closely because it fires before the final session state transition.
  - Mitigation: Keep the seam explicit and cover both success/error behavior with manager tests.
- Risk: Slow hook handlers could delay turn completion if internal hook execution is awaited.
  - Mitigation: Keep scope narrow and make the timing explicit in review/PR discussion.

target.title: feat(approvals): support ID prefix matching and reply-to-message for /approve
target.body:
## Summary

When using OpenClaw through chat platforms like Telegram, approving exec requests is unnecessarily painful — you have to copy and paste the entire UUID to approve a command. This PR makes the `/approve` flow much more practical for chat-based usage:

- **ID prefix matching**: `/approve f0c7503f allow-once` now works by matching the prefix against pending approvals (similar to git short SHAs). If the prefix is ambiguous, a clear error lists the full conflicting IDs for easy disambiguation.
- **Reply-to-message**: Replying directly to an approval request message with `/approve allow-once` (no ID needed) automatically extracts the approval ID from the message body.
- Updated the approval request hint text to inform users about both new features.

## Motivation

On desktop or web, copying a UUID is tolerable. But on mobile chat clients (Telegram, WhatsApp, etc.), selecting and copying a UUID from a message is error-prone and frustrating. The two most natural interactions — typing a short prefix or simply replying to the message — both failed with unhelpful errors before this change.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/gateway/exec-approval-manager.ts` | Added `resolveId()` method for prefix-based ID resolution; filters grace-period (already-resolved) entries from both exact and prefix match paths |
| `src/gateway/server-methods/exec-approval.ts` | Updated `exec.approval.resolve` handler to use prefix resolution with ambiguity detection; shows full IDs in error messages |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-approve.ts` | Support single-token `/approve <decision>` and extract ID from `ReplyToBody`; regex accepts arbitrary ID formats (not just hex UUIDs) |
| `src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts` | Updated hint text in approval request message |
| `src/gateway/exec-approval-manager.test.ts` | New: 8 tests for prefix resolution, grace-period filtering |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-approve.test.ts` | New: 15 tests for parser and reply-body extraction |

## Test plan

- [x] All new and existing approval-related tests pass (77 tests)
- [x] `pnpm tsgo` type check passes
- [x] Pre-commit lint hooks pass
- [x] Manually tested `/approve <prefix> allow-once` in Telegram with a short ID prefix
- [x] Manually tested replying to an approval request message with `/approve allow-once`
- [x] Manually verified ambiguous prefix returns descriptive error with full IDs

target.title: fix(docker): Change ownership of cache directory
target.body:

target.title: feat: pass requesterSenderId and senderIsOwner to ChannelAgentToolFactory
target.body:
## Summary

Pass trusted sender context (`requesterSenderId` and `senderIsOwner`) to `ChannelAgentToolFactory`, enabling channel plugins to implement sender-aware access control in their agent tools.

## Problem

`listChannelAgentTools()` only passes `{ cfg }` to channel agent tool factories, while the immediately following `createOpenClawTools()` call in the same function receives the full sender context. This gap means channel-registered agent tools cannot distinguish between different senders.

## Changes

**3 files changed, +17 / -3**

### `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts`
- Extended `ChannelAgentToolFactory` params type to include `requesterSenderId?: string | null` and `senderIsOwner?: boolean`

### `src/agents/channel-tools.ts`
- Updated `listChannelAgentTools` function signature to accept the new fields
- These are already forwarded to the factory via `entry(params)`

### `src/agents/pi-tools.ts`
- Pass `options?.senderId` and `options?.senderIsOwner` from the existing `options` object into `listChannelAgentTools()`

## Backward Compatibility

Fully backward compatible. Existing factories that only destructure `{ cfg }` will simply ignore the new optional fields. No changes required for any existing channel plugin.

## Use Case

The DMWork channel plugin needs sender-aware agent tools where:
- Any user can query their own DM history with the bot
- Any user can query groups they are a member of
- Only the bot owner can query other users' DM history

Without sender context in the factory, the plugin must choose between `ownerOnly: true` (too restrictive) or no access control (too permissive).

Closes #58806

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `ChannelAgentToolFactory` now receives `requesterSenderId` and `senderIsOwner` from the trusted server context, enabling channel plugins to implement sender-aware access control without relying on LLM-controlled input.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at `eb197b7a2b`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main (`a002c416c7`), `listChannelAgentTools` is called with only `{ cfg }` — no sender context:

```text
$ grep "listChannelAgentTools" src/agents/pi-tools.ts
921:    ...(includeChannelTools ? listChannelAgentTools({ cfg: options?.config }) : []),
```

Channel plugins have no way to distinguish between different senders.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
grep -n "requesterSenderId\|senderIsOwner" src/agents/channel-tools.ts src/agents/pi-tools.ts src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-tool-construction-plan.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
src/agents/channel-tools.ts:33:  requesterSenderId?: string | null;
src/agents/channel-tools.ts:34:  senderIsOwner?: boolean;
src/agents/channel-tools.ts:101:  requesterSenderId?: string | null;
src/agents/channel-tools.ts:102:  senderIsOwner?: boolean;
src/agents/pi-tools.ts:856:            requesterSenderId: options?.senderId,
src/agents/pi-tools.ts:857:            senderIsOwner: options?.senderIsOwner,
src/agents/pi-tools.ts:924:          requesterSenderId: options?.senderId,
src/agents/pi-tools.ts:925:          senderIsOwner: options?.senderIsOwner,
src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts:  requesterSenderId?: string | null;
src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts:  senderIsOwner?: boolean;

 ✓ unit-fast attempt-tool-construction-plan.test.ts (20 tests) 5ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  20 passed (20)
   Duration  97ms
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The sender fields are threaded from `createOpenClawCodingTools(options)` through `listChannelAgentTools(params)` into every `ChannelAgentToolFactory` call. The `includeChannelTools` and `includeOpenClawTools` per-family guards are preserved (fixed in `eb197b7a2b`) — no regression to plugin-only/channel-only construction plans. All 20 tool construction plan tests pass.
- **What was not tested**: A live channel plugin consuming `requesterSenderId` for access control was not tested in this proof. The downstream consumer is the [openclaw-channel-octo](https://github.com/nicepkg/openclaw-channel-octo) plugin, which uses these fields to gate cross-user DM history queries. The proof here verifies the core contract (params reach the factory); plugin-side enforcement is tested in the plugin's own test suite.

## P1 Fix (ClawSweeper finding addressed)

The original diff inadvertently replaced `includeChannelTools`/`includeOpenClawTools` guards with `includeCoreTools`. Fixed in `eb197b7a2b`: channel tools remain behind `includeChannelTools`, OpenClaw tools behind `includeOpenClawTools`, while sender context is forwarded in both paths.

target.title: fix(cron): reload external store edits before reusing cache
target.body:
$## What
Cron currently trusts the in-memory job store on normal operations, so live gateways can miss external edits to `~/.openclaw/cron/jobs.json` until restart.

This patch makes `ensureLoaded()` stat the backing store and reload when the file mtime changes before reusing the cached store. That lets a running gateway see manual recovery edits like clearing `runningAtMs` or removing wedged jobs.

## Why
In production we hit a cron job stuck in `already-running` even after clearing `runningAtMs` in `jobs.json`. The live process was still consulting stale in-memory state.

## Tests
Added `src/cron/service.external-store-reload.test.ts` covering:
- external clearing of a stale `runningAtMs` marker before `cron.run`
- external deletion of a job reflected by `cron.list` and `cron.run`

Ran:
```bash
pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.unit.config.ts \
  src/cron/service.external-store-reload.test.ts \
  src/cron/service.read-ops-nonblocking.test.ts \
  src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts
```

All passed (13 tests).

## Notes
This fixes the stale cached store path. It does not address the separate issue where a manual `cron.run` can return `enqueued: true` but later fail before a run-history entry is recorded.

target.title: fix: Config Raw mode permanently disabled due to round-trip check regression
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #59330 — Control UI Config editor permanently shows "Raw mode disabled (snapshot cannot safely round-trip raw text)" since 2026.3.31.

## Root Cause

Two issues cause `shouldFallbackToStructuredRawRedaction()` to always return `true`:

### 1. `normalizeExecSafeBinProfilesInConfig` assigns `void 0` instead of deleting keys

When `safeBinProfiles`/`safeBinTrustedDirs` are empty, the normalize function assigns `undefined`:

```ts
typedExec.safeBinProfiles = ... ? normalizedProfiles : undefined;
```

This creates keys with `undefined` values. JSON-parsed objects can never have `undefined`-valued keys, so `isDeepStrictEqual()` always fails:

```ts
isDeepStrictEqual({ safeBinProfiles: undefined }, {}) // → false
```

### 2. `shouldFallbackToStructuredRawRedaction` compares against materialized config

The round-trip check compares the restored (raw-parsed) config against `snapshot.config` (the **materialized** runtime config), which contains transformations not present in the raw source:

- `~/` paths expanded to absolute paths
- Model `api` field inherited from provider level
- Auto-generated `alias` fields on model entries
- Default empty `config: {}` added to plugin entries

The comparison should use `snapshot.parsed` (the source config before materialization).

## Changes

1. **`src/config/normalize-exec-safe-bin.ts`**: Use `delete` instead of assigning `undefined` when profiles/dirs are empty.

2. **`src/config/redact-snapshot.raw.ts`**:
   - Accept optional `sourceConfig` parameter for comparison target
   - Normalize both sides with a recursive filter to strip any remaining `undefined`-valued keys

3. **`src/config/redact-snapshot.ts`**: Pass `snapshot.parsed` as `sourceConfig` to the round-trip check.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Control UI Config editor permanently disabled the Raw tab and showed "Raw mode disabled (snapshot cannot safely round-trip raw text)." since openclaw 2026.3.31. Tracked in #59330.

**Real environment tested**: Globally-installed openclaw 2026.5.18 on macOS (Darwin 25.5.0, arm64). Patch applied directly to the installed `dist/` bundles (`normalize-exec-safe-bin` + `redact-snapshot` modules) to mirror the source-level fix in this PR. Gateway restarted, Control UI opened via `openclaw dashboard`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

1. Apply the patch to the installed `dist/` bundles (mirrors this PR's source changes), then restart the running gateway so it loads the patched code:

```bash
./hotfix-toggle.sh on
pkill -f 'openclaw/dist/index.js gateway'
openclaw gateway start
```

2. Open the Control UI and switch the Config editor to Raw mode:

```bash
openclaw dashboard
```

**Evidence after fix**:

Before — Control UI Config editor with Raw tab permanently disabled (reproduces the regression in #59330):

![Control UI Config editor with Raw tab disabled and "Raw mode disabled (snapshot cannot safely round-trip raw text)." message](https://github.com/rayseling/openclaw/releases/download/proof-pr-59336/raw-mode-before.png)

After — Raw tab enabled, raw JSON5 config renders with 5 sensitive values redacted (`__OPENCLAW_REDACTED__`) as expected:

![Control UI Config editor with Raw tab enabled, showing "Raw config (JSON/JSON5)" panel with "5 secrets redacted" badge and "5 sensitive values hidden. Use the reveal button above to edit the raw config." advisory](https://github.com/rayseling/openclaw/releases/download/proof-pr-59336/raw-mode-after.png)

**Observed result after fix**: The Raw tab in the Control UI Config editor is enabled. The panel renders the redacted raw JSON5 with the "5 secrets redacted" badge and the "5 sensitive values hidden. Use the reveal button above to edit the raw config." advisory. Toggling between Form and Raw works without the fallback message. `snapshot.raw` is a string instead of `null` after the patch.

**What was not tested**: Behavior with `agents[].tools.exec.safeBinProfiles` populated (only the empty-profiles regression path that triggered the bug was exercised). The short-sensitive-value collision reported by @zhangqinghua2015 in a comment on this PR is a separate raw-redaction edge case and is intentionally out of scope here.

---

🤖 AI-assisted (Claude via OpenClaw). Fully tested locally against real config. I understand what the code does.
target.author: rayseling

target.title: fix: directly deliver completed media fallback
target.body:
## Summary

- Keep generated media tasks active until completion delivery is confirmed, so duplicate/retry checks see the task while delivery is still pending.
- Add duplicate and recent-completion guards for image, video, and music generation requests.
- Add a direct generated-media fallback when requester-agent/message-tool delivery misses the completed media, while preserving the active wake/message-tool handoff path from `upstream/main`.
- Address review follow-up by preserving concurrent distinct image prompts, keeping same-prompt duplicate guards, reporting all active image tasks, and updating the generated-media fallback docs.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts src/agents/tools/media-generate-background-shared.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-background.test.ts src/agents/image-generation-task-status.test.ts src/agents/video-generation-task-status.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.status.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.status.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json --base upstream/main`
- `pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `git merge-tree HEAD upstream/main`

## Review follow-up verification

Current head `bd363a476fa4185247f558ff691d5f69ee2bef6b` addresses the latest ClawSweeper findings from the durable review comment:

- Same-prompt image retries still return active/recent status, while distinct image prompts can start their own task.
- Recent duplicate guards now keep multiple recent media starts per session/tool so a completed prompt remains guarded after another prompt starts.
- `image_generate` `action:"status"` reports every active image task instead of hiding concurrent image work behind a single task.
- `docs/automation/tasks.md`, `docs/tools/media-overview.md`, `docs/tools/image-generation.md`, `docs/tools/music-generation.md`, and `docs/tools/video-generation.md` document the direct generated-media fallback.
- Generated-media success completions now use one direct fallback/idempotency path; if that generated-media fallback cannot confirm delivery, OpenClaw fails the handoff instead of trying a second direct send with another key.

Current-head commands after those updates:

- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/image-generation-task-status.test.ts` -> 2 files, 46 tests passed.
- `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts src/agents/tools/media-generate-background-shared.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-background.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-background.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-background.test.ts` -> 5 files, 78 tests passed.
- `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts src/agents/tools/media-generate-background-shared.test.ts` -> 2 files, 57 tests passed.
- `pnpm lint --threads=8` -> passed.
- `pnpm format -- src/agents/media-generation-task-status-shared.ts src/agents/image-generation-task-status.ts src/agents/image-generation-task-status.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.actions.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts docs/automation/tasks.md docs/tools/media-overview.md` -> passed.
- `pnpm format:check -- src/agents/media-generation-task-status-shared.ts src/agents/image-generation-task-status.ts src/agents/image-generation-task-status.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.actions.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts docs/automation/tasks.md docs/tools/media-overview.md` -> passed.
- `pnpm format:check -- src/agents/image-generation-task-status.ts` -> passed after the lint fix.
- `git diff --check` -> passed.
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json --base upstream/main` -> lanes `core`, `coreTests`, `docs`.
- `pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main` -> passed.
- `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 AUTOREVIEW_OPENCLAW_MAINTAINER_VALIDATION=1 .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` -> clean; no accepted/actionable findings reported.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Generated media completions keep the task/session delivery path active until completion delivery is confirmed. If active requester wake and requester-agent/message-tool delivery miss the generated media, the PR fallback posts one direct channel media message to the original chat. The follow-up commit also prevents duplicate image-generation task handling from collapsing distinct image prompts while preserving same-prompt duplicate guards.

Real environment tested: Live Discord transport proof was collected from this PR branch at `fe92ef21ce51888cba717edd999a4ef90772a28b` (`pnpm openclaw --help` reported `OpenClaw 2026.5.19 (fe92ef2)`) against live Discord channel transport. Target channel is redacted as `discord channel:...5762`; the live fallback message id was `1506455834782072842`. After the review-follow-up updates, current head `bd363a476fa4185247f558ff691d5f69ee2bef6b` was verified with the current-head tests/checks above. The follow-up commit did not change the direct Discord fallback transport boundary exercised by the live proof; it changed image duplicate-guard/status behavior and docs.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Live fallback proof used an inline `node --import tsx --input-type=module` proof script from the PR checkout. The script resolved command-scoped Discord secrets for the configured channel, loaded the configured Discord plugin, called `deliverSubagentAnnouncement(...)` from `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts`, forced active requester wake to return `queued:false` with `source_reply_delivery_mode_mismatch`, forced the requester-agent handoff to return no message-tool/media delivery evidence, and let the PR fallback call the real `sendMessage(...)` transport. Then I fetched the exact Discord message by id with `handleDiscordAction({ action: "fetchMessage", guildId: "<redacted>", channelId: "...5762", messageId: "1506455834782072842" })` and checked the active session mirror line with `sed -n '19p' <state>/agents/main/sessions/6c7c13d6-d515-4cf4-ad90-ba2a84ef203f.jsonl | jq ...`. After the current-head follow-up patch, I reran the focused task/status/fallback tests, formatting checks, `git diff --check`, `pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main`, and autoreview listed in Review follow-up verification.

Evidence after fix: Redacted console output from the live proof run:

```text
active requester wake:
  waitForTranscriptCommit: true
  sourceReplyDeliveryMode: message_tool_only
  steeringMode: all

requester-agent handoff:
  method: agent
  expectFinal: true
  deliver: false
  channel: discord
  to: channel:...5762
  sourceReplyDeliveryMode: message_tool_only
  idempotencyKey: PR84371-MEDIA-FALLBACK-fe92ef2-1779237455145
  mediaEvents: 1

direct fallback result:
  delivered: true
  path: direct
  phase: direct-primary
  phaseDelivered: true

sendMessage receipt:
  messageId: 1506455834782072842
  platformMessageIds: [1506455834782072842]
  parts: [{ kind: media, index: 0 }]
```

Exact live Discord readback after the fallback send:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "message": {
    "id": "1506455834782072842",
    "content": "The generated image is ready.",
    "timestampUtc": "2026-05-20T00:37:39.608Z",
    "authorBot": true,
    "attachmentCount": 1,
    "attachment": {
      "filename": "PR84371-MEDIA-FALLBACK-fe92ef2-1779237455145.png",
      "size": 91,
      "width": 1,
      "height": 1,
      "content_type": "image/png"
    }
  }
}
```

Transcript mirror proof from the active Discord session after delivery:

```json
{
  "type": "message",
  "timestamp": "2026-05-20T00:37:39.973Z",
  "role": "assistant",
  "text": "PR84371-MEDIA-FALLBACK-fe92ef2-1779237455145.png",
  "provider": "openclaw",
  "model": "delivery-mirror",
  "stopReason": "stop",
  "idempotencyKey": "PR84371-MEDIA-FALLBACK-fe92ef2-177923...generated-media-direct"
}
```

Observed result after fix: The fallback produced exactly one Discord bot message in the original channel with the fixed completion text and exactly one `image/png` attachment. The active wake/requester-agent message-tool path was attempted first and missed media, then the direct fallback delivered once and the active session mirror committed after the send. Current-head tests additionally prove same-prompt image duplicate calls are guarded, distinct image prompts start separate tasks, recent success guards survive concurrent image prompts, and status reports all active image tasks.

What was not tested: Mantis Telegram Desktop visual proof did not run because workflow `26133709129` skipped downstream proof jobs after reporting actor `fuller-stack-dev` had only read permission. This proof uses live Discord transport, live Discord readback, and the real session mirror path instead. The media generator provider itself was not called; the proof injected the completed generated-media event at the delivery boundary while the fallback transport/readback/transcript surfaces were real. Live Discord transport was not rerun after the review-follow-up commits because those commits did not change the direct fallback transport implementation exercised by the proof; current-head verification covered the changed duplicate-guard/status, single-send idempotency, and docs behavior.

target.title: feat(plugins): add model failover and terminal failure hooks
target.body:
# PR Draft — #70976 model failover hooks

## Title
`feat(plugins): add model failover and terminal failure hooks`

## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw exposes model failover and terminal all-models-failed outcomes mostly through logs, so plugins and operators cannot react in-process without log scraping.
- Why it matters: This makes alerting and automation brittle, delayed, and hard to build on top of the existing failover path.
- What changed: Added two fire-and-forget plugin hooks, `model_failover` and `model_failure_terminal`, and emitted them from the existing failover-decision and terminal before-reply failure seams.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add non-verbose fallback notices, workspace-context re-injection, or circuit-breaker/policy behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #70976
- Related #65824
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Plugins can now register `model_failover` hooks and receive structured failover decision events.
- Plugins can now register `model_failure_terminal` hooks and receive structured before-reply terminal failure events, including fallback-attempt summaries for all-models-failed outcomes.
- No default user-facing fallback messaging behavior changed.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[model error] -> [log failover decision] -> [fallback or final failure]
                     \-> plugins must scrape logs

After:
[model error] -> [log failover decision + model_failover hook] -> [fallback or final failure]
                                                               \-> [model_failure_terminal hook on before-reply collapse]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This adds new plugin observability hooks. To reduce leakage risk, `model_failure_terminal` is gated by the existing conversation-access policy for non-bundled plugins, and attempt summaries expose provider/model/reason rather than raw provider error blobs.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A (hook plumbing)
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default local test config

### Steps

1. Register a plugin hook for `model_failover` or `model_failure_terminal` using the hook runner test helpers.
2. Trigger the corresponding hook runner path or failover seam.
3. Verify the handler receives the structured event payload and that hook failures do not break the main flow.

### Expected

- `model_failover` fires on failover decisions.
- `model_failure_terminal` fires on terminal before-reply failures.
- Hook execution is fire-and-forget / fail-open.

### Actual

- Added targeted tests for both hook types and failover-observation hook emission.
- Full plugin Vitest suite passed after the change.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `model_failover` emits structured fallback telemetry from the embedded failover decision seam, and `model_failure_terminal` is blocked for non-bundled plugins unless conversation access is explicitly enabled.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS / Node 25.6.0, using the production plugin registry, production hook runner, and production embedded failover decision logger from this branch.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node --import tsx .artifacts/oc-70990-real-proof.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the local OpenClaw checkout:

```text
[agent/embedded] embedded run failover decision: runId=proof-run-1 stage=prompt decision=fallback_model reason=rate_limit from=openai-codex/gpt-5.5 profile=-
{
  "diagnostics": [
    {
      "level": "warn",
      "pluginId": "proof-terminal-blocked",
      "message": "typed hook "model_failure_terminal" blocked because non-bundled plugins must set plugins.entries.proof-terminal-blocked.hooks.allowConversationAccess=true"
    }
  ],
  "hasHooks": {
    "model_failover": true,
    "model_failure_terminal": true
  },
  "eventCount": 2,
  "events": [
    {
      "hook": "model_failover",
      "event": {
        "runId": "proof-run-1",
        "agentId": "proof-agent",
        "sessionId": "proof-session-id",
        "sessionKey": "agent:proof:direct:1",
        "provider": "openai-codex",
        "model": "gpt-5.5",
        "sourceProvider": "openai-codex",
        "sourceModel": "gpt-5.5",
        "stage": "prompt",
        "decision": "fallback_model",
        "failoverReason": "rate_limit",
        "profileFailureReason": null,
        "fallbackConfigured": true,
        "status": 429,
        "sourceRecoverable": true
      }
    },
    {
      "hook": "model_failure_terminal",
      "event": {
        "runId": "proof-run-1",
        "agentId": "proof-agent",
        "sessionId": "proof-session-id",
        "sessionKey": "agent:proof:direct:1",
        "finalMessage": "All configured models failed before reply.",
        "kind": "all_models_failed",
        "attempts": [
          { "provider": "openai-codex", "model": "gpt-5.5", "reason": "rate_limit" },
          { "provider": "openrouter", "model": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6", "reason": "overloaded" }
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The production registry rejected an ungated non-bundled `model_failure_terminal` registration, accepted the gated terminal hook, emitted one `model_failover` event with failed/current model refs plus `rate_limit`, `status: 429`, and `sourceRecoverable: true`, and emitted one `model_failure_terminal` event with all-models-failed attempt summaries.
- **What was not tested**: A live external provider outage was not forced; this proof exercises the local OpenClaw plugin registry/hook runner/failover-observation path without making network calls.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - reviewed the source seams where failover decisions and terminal failures are emitted
  - verified the new hook names are registered in the plugin hook surface
  - verified targeted tests pass for runner plumbing and failover-observation emission
  - verified the full plugin Vitest suite passes
- Edge cases checked:
  - no-op when no hook is registered
  - fail-open behavior when a hook throws
  - repeated decision emissions still invoke hooks correctly
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full-project `tsc --noEmit` on this host, because it OOMs due to an existing machine limitation
  - a live end-to-end provider outage scenario outside the test suite

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Hook payload scope may be seen as too narrow for v1 because it omits richer source-model context.
  - Mitigation: Keep this PR intentionally minimal and source-backed; richer payload fields can be added in follow-up work if maintainers want them.
- Risk: Hook emission could accidentally interfere with failover paths if blocking.
  - Mitigation: Both hooks are wired through existing fire-and-forget `runVoidHook` behavior.
- Risk: Terminal failure event content could leak too much provider detail.
  - Mitigation: `model_failure_terminal` uses the sanitized message path and structured attempt summaries instead of raw provider error blobs.

target.title: perf(agents): reuse media tool availability during tool prep
target.body:
## Summary
- Reuse the optional media tool factory plan for the image tool instead of resolving image availability separately.
- Let image/video/music generation tool factories skip their registration-time availability scan when createOpenClawTools already resolved availability.
- Use the same effective media tool config for the plan and constructed media tools.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: Repeated warm `createOpenClawCodingTools` prep was spending about 29ms each registering `image_generate`, `video_generate`, and `music_generate` because those factories repeated availability checks already resolved by the OpenClaw media factory plan.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `media-tool-availability-pr4c`, Node 25.9.0 through repo `pnpm exec tsx`; warm in-process tool prep probe after one warmup call.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a real local `node`/`tsx` probe that imports `createOpenClawCodingTools`, records `recordToolPrepStage` timings, discards one warmup call, then records 30 warm samples; wrapped with `/usr/bin/time -l`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
total p50 103.68ms, p95 110.16ms
openclaw-tools:image_generate registration p50 0.005ms
openclaw-tools:video_generate registration p50 0.002ms
openclaw-tools:music_generate registration p50 0.002ms
/usr/bin/time -l max RSS 850575360 bytes
```

Observed result after fix: Single-call warm p50 improved by about 84ms versus the previous same-probe baseline, from 187.74ms p50 to 103.68ms p50 (~45%). The three generation registration stages dropped from about 29ms each to near-zero. Remaining warm prep cost is mostly plugin-tools (~73ms p50) and image/media availability planning (~30ms p50).

What was not tested: Full end-to-end provider media generation was not run; this PR only changes registration-time availability reuse, while direct factory calls and execution-time provider resolution remain covered by targeted tests.

## Verification
- pnpm test src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts
- pnpm tsgo:core
- pnpm build
- pnpm check:changed --staged

target.title: fix: always apply Anthropic beta headers wrapper for OAuth token support
target.body:
## Summary

- Always apply `createAnthropicBetaHeadersWrapper` for Anthropic providers, not just when `resolveAnthropicBetas()` returns non-empty
- This ensures the `oauth-2025-04-20` beta is injected for OAuth tokens (`sk-ant-oat-*`) even when `context1m` is configured via model-level `headers` instead of agent extra params

## Problem

When `context-1m-2025-08-07` is set in `models.providers.anthropic.models[].headers` (model-level) rather than in `agents.defaults.models[].params.context1m` (extra params), `resolveAnthropicBetas()` returns `undefined`, so `createAnthropicBetaHeadersWrapper` is never applied. The pi-ai SDK detects the `sk-ant-oat` prefix and uses `Authorization: Bearer`, but without the required `oauth-2025-04-20` beta header, Anthropic rejects with:

```
HTTP 401 authentication_error: OAuth authentication is currently not supported.
```

The fix from #19789 correctly handles this *inside* the wrapper, but the wrapper was gated behind a non-empty betas check that prevented it from running.

## Changes

**`extra-params.ts`**: Always apply the beta headers wrapper for `provider === "anthropic"`, passing an empty array when no explicit betas are configured. The wrapper's internal OAuth detection then runs unconditionally.

**`pi-embedded-runner-extraparams.test.ts`**: Updated existing test and added new test for OAuth tokens without `context1m` configured.

## Test plan

- [x] All 67 existing tests pass
- [x] New test: OAuth betas injected for `sk-ant-oat` tokens even without `context1m`
- [x] Verified fix resolves the issue on a live deployment

Fixes #41444
Related: #19769, #19789

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: telegram: add forum topic deletion support to message delete action
target.body:
## Summary
- add first-class Telegram forum topic deletion path to `message action=delete`
- keep backward compatibility for message deletion via `messageId`
- accept `threadId`/`topicId` (and parse `:topic:<id>` targets) for topic deletion
- add Telegram transport helper `deleteForumTopicTelegram`
- update docs and tests for delete message/topic routing

## Details
- `src/channels/plugins/actions/telegram.ts`
  - `delete` now routes to `deleteMessage` when `messageId` is provided
  - otherwise routes to `deleteForumTopic` when `threadId`/`topicId` (or topic-qualified target) is provided
  - throws clear error when neither path has required id
- `src/agents/tools/telegram-actions.ts`
  - added `deleteForumTopic` action handling (gated with `createForumTopic` toggle)
- `src/telegram/send.ts`
  - added `deleteForumTopicTelegram(chatId, topicId, opts)` with chat normalization + retry/diag wrappers
- `src/agents/tools/message-tool.ts`
  - added `topicId` schema alias for forum topic id input
- `docs/channels/telegram.md`
  - documented `deleteForumTopic` and `delete` behavior for `messageId` vs `threadId/topicId`

## Tests run
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/channels/plugins/actions/telegram.ts src/agents/tools/telegram-actions.ts src/telegram/send.ts src/agents/tools/telegram-actions.test.ts src/channels/plugins/actions/actions.test.ts src/telegram/send.test.ts src/telegram/send.proxy.test.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.ts src/telegram/send.test-harness.ts docs/channels/telegram.md`
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/channels/plugins/actions/actions.test.ts src/agents/tools/telegram-actions.test.ts src/telegram/send.proxy.test.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/telegram/send.test.ts -t "deleteForumTopicTelegram|createForumTopicTelegram|deletes a forum topic|respects createForumTopic gating for deleteForumTopic|delete maps to deleteForumTopic|delete maps to deleteMessage|rejects delete when neither messageId nor topic/thread id is provided"`
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/telegram/send.proxy.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts`

## Note
- A full run of `src/telegram/send.test.ts` includes 2 pre-existing flaky failures around retry timing (`retries on transient errors with retry_after`, `retries when grammY network envelope message includes failed-after wording`) unrelated to this change.

target.title: docs: define hook surface ownership and middleware guidance

## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: the docs still blur file-based hooks, plugin lifecycle hooks, and plugin-managed legacy hook registration into one generic “hooks” concept.
- Why it matters: that ambiguity keeps driving middleware/security proposals toward the wrong surface and makes `api.registerHook(...)` look like the default API instead of the compatibility bridge.
- What changed: `docs/automation/hooks.md` now defines the operator-automation boundary for HOOK-style hooks, and `docs/tools/plugin.md` now makes `api.on(...)` the preferred runtime lifecycle/middleware surface while documenting `api.registerHook(...)` as the legacy internal-hook bridge.

target.title: fix(hooks): deliver internal hook replies on replyable surfaces
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `event.messages` was documented as a generic user-reply channel, but only `/new` and `/reset` actually drained those messages.
- Why it matters: `message:received` hooks could push reply text that was silently dropped, while other lifecycle hooks looked supported even though they had no safe reply target.
- What changed: added a shared internal hook reply-drain helper, kept reset/new command behavior, and now deliver `message:received` hook replies when a routable reply target exists.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not make bootstrap, preprocessed/transcribed, sent, or other lifecycle-only hooks auto-reply.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39688
- Related #40185

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Internal hook replies pushed to `event.messages` are now delivered on `message:received` when OpenClaw has a reply-capable routing target.
- Docs now explicitly limit `event.messages` auto-delivery to reply-capable surfaces instead of implying it works for all hooks.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): internal hooks / inbound reply routing
- Relevant config (redacted): default hook + routeReply test harnesses

### Steps

1. Register an internal hook that pushes text into `event.messages`.
2. Trigger it from `/new` or `message:received`.
3. Verify the reply is routed only when a safe reply target exists.

### Expected

- Reply-capable surfaces route the accumulated hook messages.
- Lifecycle-only surfaces remain non-replying unless they gain an explicit delivery contract later.

### Actual

- Before this fix, `message:received` hook replies were silently dropped.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: `commands-core` still routes reset/new hook replies; `dispatch-from-config` now routes `message:received` hook replies on routable surfaces and skips non-routable ones.
- Edge cases checked: non-routable surfaces do not auto-send hook replies; docs/comments now match that contract.
- What you did **not** verify: live messaging-provider delivery outside the existing routeReply test harnesses.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert `fix(hooks): deliver internal hook replies on replyable surfaces`
- Files/config to restore: `src/auto-reply/reply/internal-hook-replies.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-core.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts`, `docs/automation/hooks.md`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: duplicate or unexpected hook replies on inbound turns, or `message:received` hook replies not routing when they should.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `message:received` hook replies use the same route-reply path as other provider-routed responses, so a bad target selection would misroute the hook message.
  - Mitigation: the helper uses existing inbound routing fields (`OriginatingTo`, `To`, channel normalization) and adds explicit tests for routable and non-routable surfaces.

target.title: feat(feishu): add delete tools for Bitable fields and records
target.body:
## Problem

The existing Bitable tools only support create/read/update operations. There are no tools for deleting fields or records, even though the underlying Lark SDK APIs are available and already used internally (e.g., in \cleanupNewBitable\).

This means the LLM cannot:
- Remove unwanted fields when repurposing an existing table
- Delete records individually or in batch
- Clean up template fields from a shared table

The LLM incorrectly reports 'the API does not support deleting fields' when in fact it's just missing the tool registration.

## Solution

Add three new tools that expose the existing Lark SDK delete APIs:

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| \ eishu_bitable_delete_record\ | Delete a single record by ID |
| \ eishu_bitable_batch_delete_records\ | Delete up to 500 records at once |
| \ eishu_bitable_delete_field\ | Delete a field by field_id (primary field protected) |

All three follow the same patterns as existing tools:
- Use \ensureLarkSuccess\ for error handling
- Support multi-account via \^GccountId\ parameter
- Return structured JSON responses

## Notes

- Primary fields cannot be deleted (enforced by the Feishu API)
- Batch delete is capped at 500 records per call (enforced by schema)
- No breaking changes to existing tools

target.title: fix(skills): restore executable bit on bundled whisper script + release-time check
target.body:
## Problem

The bundled Whisper skill shell script (`skills/openai-whisper-api/scripts/transcribe.sh`) lost its executable bit at some point, causing `EACCES` failures when the skill tries to run it (fixes #9303).

## Solution

- Restore the executable bit (`chmod +x`) on `transcribe.sh`
- Add a release-time check (`collectSkillShellScriptExecutableErrors`) that scans all `skills/*/scripts/*.sh` files and fails the build if any are missing the executable bit — prevents this class of regression permanently

## Testing

- 10 tests pass including 2 new tests for the executable-bit checker (non-executable detection + executable acceptance)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Bundled OpenAI Whisper and video-frame skill helper scripts must be executable so direct helper invocation does not fail with EACCES.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head 2204a78702 on macOS with bash/zsh, Node, curl, and Homebrew ffmpeg. The Whisper request used a local OpenAI-compatible HTTP stub so no audio or API key left the machine.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Verified git modes and execute bits, ran `skills/openai-whisper-api/scripts/transcribe.sh` against the local stub, generated a tiny video with ffmpeg, then ran `skills/video-frames/scripts/frame.sh` to extract a frame.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the PR checkout:

```text
$ git rev-parse --short HEAD
2204a78702
$ git ls-tree HEAD skills/openai-whisper-api/scripts/transcribe.sh skills/video-frames/scripts/frame.sh
100755 blob 2a4fd20afa6104f0624a6f60ed82e4a81671ec1d	skills/openai-whisper-api/scripts/transcribe.sh
100755 blob 31b3adb34c068732d04cb0d951d519338458df98	skills/video-frames/scripts/frame.sh
$ test -x skills/openai-whisper-api/scripts/transcribe.sh && test -x skills/video-frames/scripts/frame.sh && echo executable
executable
$ OPENAI_API_KEY=redacted OPENAI_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:18743/v1 skills/openai-whisper-api/scripts/transcribe.sh $tmp/sample.wav --out $tmp/transcript.txt
/var/folders/wk/9gjf5c_940g4npdlmg0r3h8c0000gn/T/tmp.9p5hDDgFuQ/transcript.txt
$ sed -n '1p' $tmp/transcript.txt
transcribed by local proof endpoint
$ command -v ffmpeg
/opt/homebrew/bin/ffmpeg
$ ffmpeg -hide_banner -loglevel error -y -f lavfi -i testsrc=size=32x32:rate=1:duration=1 $tmp/sample.mp4
$ skills/video-frames/scripts/frame.sh $tmp/sample.mp4 --index 0 --out $tmp/frame.jpg
/var/folders/wk/9gjf5c_940g4npdlmg0r3h8c0000gn/T/tmp.9p5hDDgFuQ/frame.jpg
$ file $tmp/frame.jpg
/var/folders/wk/9gjf5c_940g4npdlmg0r3h8c0000gn/T/tmp.9p5hDDgFuQ/frame.jpg: JPEG image data, JFIF standard 1.02, aspect ratio, density 1x1, segment length 16, comment: "Lavc62.11.100", baseline, precision 8, 32x32, components 3
$ test -s $tmp/frame.jpg && echo frame-created

```

- **Observed result after fix:** Both helper scripts are recorded as 100755 and pass `test -x`; the Whisper helper wrote the expected transcript text from the local endpoint; the video-frame helper wrote a non-empty 32x32 JPEG frame.
- **What was not tested:** A live OpenAI API transcription request was not sent; the local OpenAI-compatible endpoint was used to exercise the helper's direct execution and upload path without external data transfer.

target.title: feat(feishu): auto-grant requester permission when creating Bitable
target.body:
## Problem

When a Bitable is created via  eishu_bitable_create_app, the owning app (bot) is the sole collaborator. The requesting Feishu user has **no edit permission** on the newly created table, which forces them to manually request access or rely on the  eishu_perm tool.

This is inconsistent with  eishu_doc (create action), which already auto-grants edit permission to the trusted requester after document creation.

## Solution

Mirror the existing permission-grant pattern from docx.ts createDoc into  itable.ts createApp:

1. **createApp() function**: Added optional options.grantToRequester / options.requesterOpenId parameters. After bitable creation and cleanup, calls drive.permissionMember.create() with type  itable to grant the requester edit access. Default behavior is to grant (matching docx).

2. **CreateAppSchema**: Added grant_to_requester optional boolean field so callers can opt out if needed.

3. **Tool registration**: Switched  eishu_bitable_create_app from the egisterBitableTool helper to direct ^Gpi.registerTool() call to access ctx.requesterSenderId (the trusted requester identity from the message context), same pattern as  eishu_doc.

## Response format

The tool response now includes permission status fields (when granting is enabled):

- equester_permission_added: boolean
- equester_open_id: string (when available)
- equester_perm_type: 'edit'
- equester_permission_skipped_reason: string (when identity unavailable)
- equester_permission_error: string (when grant fails)

## Notes

- Only affects Feishu channel requests (non-Feishu channels skip the grant since equesterSenderId is unavailable)
- Permission grant failure is non-fatal (reported in response but doesn't throw)
- Requires drive:permission scope on the Feishu app

target.title: Security: add tirith pre-exec command scanning
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** OpenClaw's agent executes arbitrary shell commands via the exec tool. A prompt injection, malicious skill, or hallucinated command could pipe remote scripts to bash, curl homograph URLs, or use hidden Unicode — all bypassing the existing allowlist/approval flow.
- **Why it matters:** The exec tool's existing security (allowlist + user approval) operates at the command-name level. It cannot detect _content-level_ threats like Cyrillic lookalike characters in URLs, pipe-to-shell patterns, or ANSI terminal injection within approved commands.
- **What changed:** Adds an optional pre-exec security layer using [tirith](https://github.com/sheeki03/tirith) — a terminal security tool with 66 detection rules across 11 categories. Before any command executes, tirith analyzes it and returns `allow`, `warn`, or `block`. Two hook points: gateway/sandbox (pre-dispatch) and node (post-dispatch, after platform resolution). Configurable via `tools.exec.commandSecurity`.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to the existing allowlist, approval flow, or obfuscation detector. tirith is additive — if not installed, everything works exactly as before (fail-open by default).

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: [Discussion #10317 — Integrate tirith for pre-exec command security validation](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/discussions/10317)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- **New config key:** `tools.exec.commandSecurity` with fields `enabled` (default: true), `failOpen` (default: true), `timeoutMs` (default: 5000), `tirithPath` (default: "tirith").
- **When tirith is installed:** commands are scanned before execution. Blocked commands show an explanation. Warned commands trigger the approval UI with a security warning.
- **When tirith is NOT installed:** no change in behavior — fail-open by default, commands proceed normally.
- **Elevated/sandbox paths:** warn findings are escalated to block (no approval mechanism available on these paths).

## What is tirith?

[tirith](https://github.com/sheeki03/tirith) is a terminal security tool that intercepts shell commands and analyzes them before execution. It runs entirely locally — no network calls, no telemetry, no cloud dependency. Sub-millisecond overhead on clean commands.

**66 detection rules across 11 categories:**

| Category | What it catches |
|----------|----------------|
| **Homograph attacks** | Cyrillic/Greek lookalikes in hostnames, punycode domains, mixed-script labels, confusable TLDs |
| **Terminal injection** | ANSI escape sequences, bidi overrides, zero-width characters, unicode tags, variation selectors |
| **Pipe-to-shell** | `curl \| bash`, `wget \| sh`, every source-to-sink variant including `python <(curl ...)`, `eval $(wget ...)` |
| **Command safety** | Dotfile overwrites, archive extraction to sensitive paths, cloud metadata access |
| **Insecure transport** | Plain HTTP piped to shell, disabled TLS verification (`curl -k`), shortened URLs |
| **Environment** | Proxy hijacking, sensitive env exports, interpreter hijack, shell injection via env |
| **Config file security** | AI config injection, suspicious indicators, MCP server security issues |
| **Ecosystem threats** | Git clone typosquats, untrusted Docker registries, pip/npm URL installs |
| **Path analysis** | Non-ASCII paths, homoglyphs in paths, double-encoding |
| **Rendered content** | Hidden CSS/color content, HTML attributes, markdown comments with hidden instructions |
| **Cloaking detection** | Server-side cloaking (different content for bots vs browsers) |

**Why this matters for OpenClaw:** The agent can execute arbitrary shell commands. A prompt injection or hallucinated command could pipe a remote script to bash, or curl a homograph URL that visually mimics a legitimate domain but resolves to an attacker's server. tirith catches these before execution.

**Beyond command scanning**, tirith also offers (not part of this PR, but available to users):
- **MCP server** with 7 tools — agents can proactively call `tirith_check_command`, `tirith_check_url`, `tirith_scan_file`, `tirith_verify_mcp_config`, and more
- **Config file scanning** — detects prompt injection and hidden payloads in 50+ AI config file patterns (`.cursorrules`, `CLAUDE.md`, `.mcp.json`, etc.)
- **Hidden content detection** — CSS hiding, color hiding, PDF sub-pixel text invisible to humans but readable by LLMs
- **Cloaking detection** — compares server responses across 6 user-agents to detect differential content serving
- **One-command setup** — `tirith setup openclaw` installs the integration automatically

Install: `brew install sheeki03/tap/tirith` · `cargo install tirith` · `npm i -g @anthropic/tirith` · [more options](https://github.com/sheeki03/tirith#install)

## How it works

```
Command entered → tirith check --json --shell <posix|powershell|cmd> -- "<command>"
                        │
                   Exit 0 → allow (no output, zero overhead)
                   Exit 1 → block (JSON findings in stdout)
                   Exit 2 → warn  (JSON findings in stdout)
                   ENOENT → tirith not installed → allow (fail-open)
                   Timeout → allow (fail-open, configurable)
```

**Two hook points:**

| Hook | Location | Shell detection | Rationale |
|------|----------|----------------|-----------|
| Gateway/sandbox | `bash-tools.exec.ts` (pre-dispatch) | `process.platform` → posix/powershell | Platform known at dispatch time |
| Node | `bash-tools.exec-host-node.ts` (post-dispatch) | `nodeInfo.platform` → posix/cmd | Platform only known after `listNodes()` |

**Warn escalation:** On paths without an approval mechanism (elevated-bypass gateway, sandbox), warn is escalated to block. Rationale: if tirith detects something suspicious and there's no way to show the user, the safe default is to block.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No` — tirith runs as a local child process, same as existing tools
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` — tirith analysis is entirely local
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes` — adds a pre-exec security check that can block or warn on commands
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- **Risk + mitigation:** The new check can _prevent_ command execution (block) or _add warnings_ (warn). It cannot execute commands itself. tirith is invoked via `execFile` (not `exec`) — the command string is passed as an argv element, not shell-interpolated. The check runs with a configurable timeout (default 5s) and fail-open default, so a misbehaving tirith binary cannot hang the exec pipeline. If tirith is not installed (ENOENT), the check silently allows.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.3 (also tested conceptually for Windows gateway/node paths)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 22.x
- Model/provider: N/A (infrastructure change)
- Integration/channel: exec tool (gateway, sandbox, node)
- Relevant config: `tools.exec.commandSecurity.enabled: true`

### Steps

1. Install tirith: `brew install sheeki03/tap/tirith`
2. Run OpenClaw with this PR
3. Execute a command that tirith would block (e.g., a URL with Cyrillic characters resembling a legitimate domain)
4. Execute a command that tirith would warn (e.g., a shortened URL)
5. Execute a normal command: `ls -la`
6. Uninstall tirith and verify commands still work (fail-open)

### Expected

- Step 3: Command blocked with homograph finding
- Step 4: Approval UI shows security warning
- Step 5: Command executes normally (exit 0 = allow)
- Step 6: All commands proceed (ENOENT = allow with failOpen=true)

### Actual

- All scenarios verified via unit tests (20 tests) and integration tests (8 tests)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

28 new tests passing:
- `src/security/command-security.test.ts` — 20 unit tests covering all exit code paths, JSON parsing, failOpen behavior, shell flag forwarding, env passthrough, tilde expansion
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.approval-id.test.ts` — 8 integration tests covering gateway block, gateway warn-to-approval, elevated bypass escalation, enabled=false skip, node block, node warn-to-approval, node shell detection

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** All 28 test cases pass. Manually tested with tirith 0.2.6 installed: block/warn/allow paths work correctly. Tested ENOENT path by renaming tirith binary.
- **Edge cases checked:** Invalid JSON in tirith stdout, empty findings array on warn, timeout handling, unknown exit codes, tilde expansion in tirithPath, Windows shell flag resolution (powershell for gateway, cmd for node).
- **What I did not verify:** Actual Windows gateway/node execution (no Windows environment available). Windows shell flags are covered by unit tests and match OpenClaw's existing `shell-utils.ts` and `node-shell.ts` conventions.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — fail-open by default, no behavior change when tirith is not installed
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — new optional `tools.exec.commandSecurity` config key
- Migration needed? `No` — zero-config activation, works out of the box
- Upgrade steps: Install tirith (`brew install sheeki03/tap/tirith` or `cargo install tirith`), optionally configure `tools.exec.commandSecurity` in config

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- **How to disable/revert quickly:** Set `tools.exec.commandSecurity.enabled: false` in config, or uninstall tirith (ENOENT = allow)
- **Files/config to restore:** No files to restore — the feature is additive
- **Known bad symptoms:** If tirith binary hangs, commands will be delayed up to `timeoutMs` (default 5s), then allowed (fail-open). If tirith returns unexpected exit codes, behavior depends on `failOpen` setting.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** tirith binary not found on PATH in non-interactive shell environments (e.g., OpenClaw running as a service)
  - **Mitigation:** The integration resolves login-shell PATH before invoking tirith, matching OpenClaw's existing PATH recovery for other tools. Users can also set `tirithPath` to an absolute path.
- **Risk:** tirith false positives blocking legitimate commands
  - **Mitigation:** tirith has a conservative rule set (66 rules, each with severity tiers). Block is reserved for high-confidence threats (homograph attacks, pipe-to-interpreter). Lower-confidence findings produce warn, which shows in the approval UI rather than blocking. Users can also configure tirith's policy system to allowlist specific patterns.
