target.title: fix(agents): credit delivered subagent completions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `expectsCompletionMessage` subagent cleanup treated the later parent announce as the only delivery signal, so a parent `NO_REPLY` could retry even after the requester thread already had a committed delivery mirror.
- Solution: when a completion announce reports no visible delivery, lifecycle cleanup now checks the requester session for a matching `openclaw/delivery-mirror` assistant message before retrying.
- What changed: matching prior delivery mirrors clear the delivery error path, preserve the mirror timestamp as `completionDeliveredAt`, finalize cleanup as delivered, and skip pending-final retry bookkeeping.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no channel delivery APIs, prompt instructions, message-tool requirements, config defaults, or provider behavior changed.

## Motivation

- Fixes the bookkeeping failure reported in #84270, where Slack already had the subagent completion reply but the registry retried the completion announce until `retry-limit`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #84270
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: completion cleanup now credits an already-committed requester delivery mirror instead of retrying a parent `NO_REPLY` announce.
- Real environment tested: local macOS source checkout, Node/Vitest test harness for the subagent lifecycle controller.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts`; `pnpm check:changed`; `git diff --check`.
- Evidence after fix: copied terminal output from the full lifecycle file run: `Test Files  2 passed (2)` and `Tests  46 passed (46)`.
- Observed result after fix: the regression asserts `chat.history` is queried for the requester session, `completionDeliveredAt`/`completionAnnouncedAt` are set from the mirror timestamp, `lastAnnounceDeliveryError` is cleared, `pendingFinalDelivery` and `announceRetryCount` remain unset, detached task delivery is marked `delivered`, and `logAnnounceGiveUp` is not called.
- What was not tested: live Slack workspace delivery with a real gateway session.
- Before evidence: the issue log showed `Subagent announce give up (retry-limit) ... retries=3`; the pre-existing focused lifecycle proof covered the old failure bookkeeping path with `completion agent did not produce a visible reply` / `UNAVAILABLE: requester wake failed` style delivery errors.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: cleanup finalized based on `didAnnounce = delivery.delivered` from the later parent-mediated announce only, and did not consult requester-session delivery mirror evidence from the child’s already-committed visible reply.
- Missing detection / guardrail: lifecycle tests covered invisible completion announce failure and retry state, but not the case where a matching requester delivery mirror already proved user-visible delivery.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: parent announce returns no visible delivery while the requester session already contains a matching `delivery-mirror` completion reply.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the lifecycle retry/finalize decision directly without requiring a live Slack workspace.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Subagent completion runs that already delivered a visible reply to the requester thread should stop retrying/giving up on the later completion announce when the parent correctly replies `NO_REPLY`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
child delivered reply -> requester delivery mirror exists -> parent announce NO_REPLY -> retry/give-up

After:
child delivered reply -> requester delivery mirror exists -> parent announce NO_REPLY -> cleanup delivered
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

Security/runtime controls unchanged: message-tool-only delivery policy and channel send enforcement remain in the existing delivery path. This patch only credits a persisted `openclaw` `delivery-mirror` in the same requester session after runtime delivery has already committed it.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local checkout
- Runtime/container: Node/Vitest via repo wrapper
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): mocked requester session history for Slack-like completion lifecycle
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Simulate a completed `expectsCompletionMessage` subagent run.
2. Make the parent announce report `delivered: false` with `completion agent did not produce a visible reply`.
3. Return a matching requester `delivery-mirror` message from `chat.history`.

### Expected

- Cleanup finalizes as delivered and does not schedule pending final delivery retries.

### Actual

- The new regression passes with delivered status, cleared error state, no pending final delivery, no retry count, and no give-up log.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Verification run output:

```text
git diff --check
# passed with no output

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  46 passed (46)

pnpm check:changed
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
runtime-sidecar-loaders: local runtime sidecar loaders look OK.
Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: matching requester delivery mirror credits a failed parent announce; existing failure bookkeeping still remains covered.
- Edge cases checked: matching uses normalized visible text and requires `role: assistant`, `provider: openclaw`, and `model: delivery-mirror` in the same requester session history.
- What you did **not** verify: live Slack end-to-end delivery.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

AI-assisted: yes.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: accidentally crediting the wrong transcript entry.
  - Mitigation: only requester-session `openclaw/delivery-mirror` assistant messages with matching normalized completion text are credited.
- Out-of-scope follow-up: live Slack proof for the original redacted production run remains outside this small code/test patch.

target.title: Control UI + auto-reply: strip leaked agent scaffolding and metadata
target.body:
## Summary

Three related bugs where Control UI (and any user-facing surface that shares `stripInboundMetadata`) leaks message internals that should already have been stripped:

1. `renderGroupedMessage` was extracting text directly from `normalizedMessage.content` instead of going through `extractText`/`processMessageText`, so `<think>`/`<final>` scaffolding from Gemini-family models and inbound metadata sentinels were rendered verbatim in the chat bubble.
2. `stripInboundMetadata` only handled the structured JSON-block sentinels. Per-line `System:` / `System (untrusted):` event prefixes emitted by `session-system-events.ts`, and the paired "An async command you ran earlier has completed." / "Current time: …" internal agent instructions, still leaked into user-visible text.
3. With (1) and (2) fixed, user turns whose text consisted entirely of now-stripped metadata still rendered an orphan "You · 12:34" bubble (avatar + sender label + timestamp + delete button, no content) because `renderMessageGroup` had no group-level suppression analogous to the per-message check inside `renderGroupedMessage`.

## Changes

- `ui/src/ui/chat/message-extract.ts`: export `processMessageText` so other renderers can share the same per-role strip contract.
- `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts`:
  - route `renderGroupedMessage`'s text extraction through `processMessageText` (assistant → `stripThinkingTags`, user → `stripInboundMetadata(stripEnvelope(...))`, other → `stripEnvelope`).
  - add a group-level emptiness precheck in `renderMessageGroup` so a group whose inner messages all render empty returns `nothing` instead of leaving an orphan avatar/footer behind.
- `src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.ts`:
  - add `SYSTEM_EVENT_LINE_RE` and an `INTERNAL_INSTRUCTION_LINE_PREFIXES` list.
  - extend the fast-path `SENTINEL_FAST_RE` to include `System:` / `System (untrusted):` and the instruction prefixes so these variants still trigger the slow parse.
  - in the main loop, drop lines matching either pattern. Only line-anchored matches are stripped so prose like "Operating System: Linux" is preserved.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test ui/src/ui/chat/message-extract.test.ts` — new `processMessageText` suite covers the empty-`<think></think>` + `<final>` variant I actually hit with `gemini-3-flash-preview`, plus the standard paired shape and plain pass-through for assistant/user roles.
- [x] `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.test.ts` — new `per-line system events and internal instructions` suite covers consecutive `System (untrusted):` exec lines, trusted `System:` lines, the async-command / current-time instruction pair, and guards against stripping the inline "Operating System: Linux" prose variant.
- [x] `pnpm tsgo`, `pnpm check`, `pnpm check:import-cycles`, `pnpm check:madge-import-cycles` — all green on the PR branch (run via `scripts/committer` pre-commit).
- [x] Manual smoke on a live self-hosted OpenClaw instance with Gemini 3 Flash Preview: `<final>…</final>` segments no longer render verbatim, `System (untrusted):` / async-command lines no longer surface in user bubbles, and orphan "You · HH:MM" empty groups no longer appear after metadata-only turns. Existing `<think>` reasoning blocks still surface correctly in the dedicated reasoning panel.

No config schema, plugin-SDK surface, or build output shape was changed.

target.title: feat(memory-core): add multi-strategy chunking support
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Memory search chunking was hardcoded to a single fixed-size strategy, offering no flexibility for different document types (markdown-heavy, narrative, multi-lingual, hierarchical) that benefit from different segmentation approaches.
- **Why it matters:** Poor chunking quality directly degrades memory search recall and precision. Different document structures require different splitting strategies — fixed-size token windows lose semantic boundaries, while heading-aware or sentence-aware splits preserve meaning at chunk borders.
- **What changed:**
  - Introduced a pluggable chunking strategy framework in `src/memory-host-sdk/host/chunking/` with 6 strategies: 
    - `fixed-size` (original, default)
    - `markdown-heading`
    - `sentence`
    - `semantic` (embedding-based cosine distance breakpoints)
    - `lumber` (LLM paragraph-level content-shift detection, per arXiv:2406.17526)
    - `hichunk` (hierarchical multi-level LLM-driven semantic segmentation, arXiv:2509.11552)
  - Added a `ChunkingStrategy` interface (`types.ts`), a factory function `resolveChunkingStrategy` (`resolve.ts`), and per-strategy implementations with configurable parameters and defaults.
  - Extended the config surface: `agents.defaults.memorySearch.chunking.strategy` and per-strategy fields (`tokens`, `overlap`, `maxDepth`, `maxTokens`, `targetTokens`, `overlapSentences`, `bufferSize`, `breakpointPercentileThreshold`, `theta`, `completionModel`, `windowSize`, `lineMaxLen`, `maxLevel`, `recurrentType`) in types (`types.tools.ts`), zod schema (`zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts`), and schema help (`schema.help.ts`).
  - Updated `resolveChunkingConfig` in `src/agents/memory-search.ts` to build strategy-specific config with defaults and clamping.
  - Updated `manager-embedding-ops.ts` to resolve the configured strategy at index time and route through `strategy.chunk()` instead of the old `chunkMarkdown()` directly.
  - Updated `manager-reindex-state.ts` with `chunkingConfigDiffers()` to detect per-strategy config changes and trigger full reindex when needed, with backward-compat for legacy `chunkTokens`/`chunkOverlap` meta.
  - Updated `manager-sync-ops.ts` to persist `chunkingConfig` in index meta alongside legacy `chunkTokens`/`chunkOverlap`.
  - Added comprehensive unit tests in `chunking.test.ts` covering all 6 strategies, helper functions, and the factory.
  - Added reindex-state tests for chunking config drift detection across strategies and legacy meta compat.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** The embedding pipeline, vector/FTS storage schema, query/search path, hybrid scoring, and session sync logic are untouched. The default behavior (`fixed-size` with existing defaults) is preserved for all existing users.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a new feature, not a bug fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — new feature, not a regression fix. New test coverage is included.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/memory-host-sdk/host/chunking/chunking.test.ts`, `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - Each strategy produces valid `MemoryChunk[]` with correct `startLine`/`endLine`/`text`/`hash`/`embeddingInput`
  - Factory resolves correct strategy and throws on missing dependencies (provider for semantic, completionFn for lumber/hichunk)
  - Reindex state correctly detects config drift per-strategy and handles legacy meta backward-compat
  - Helper functions (sentence splitting, heading parsing, LLM answer parsing, etc.) produce expected outputs
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Unit tests exercise each strategy's core splitting logic and the config resolution/reindex detection in isolation without requiring a running embedding provider or LLM.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing `manager-reindex-state.test.ts` was extended with chunking-specific cases.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests **are** added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config key: `agents.defaults.memorySearch.chunking.strategy` — accepts `"fixed-size"` (default), `"markdown-heading"`, `"sentence"`, `"semantic"`, `"lumber"`, or `"hichunk"`.
- New strategy-specific config keys under `agents.defaults.memorySearch.chunking.*` (see schema help for documentation of each).
- `lumber` and `hichunk` strategies require `chunking.completionModel` to be set; config resolution throws if missing.
  - `lumber` using any general LLM as `chunking.completionModel`
  - `hichunk` needs to serve the model released by arXiv:2509.11552 as `chunking.completionModel`
- `semantic` strategy requires a working embedding provider (using `agents.defaults.memorySearch.model` as embedding provider).
- Changing the strategy or its parameters triggers a full memory reindex on next sync.
- Default behavior is **unchanged**: existing users who have not set `chunking.strategy` continue to use `fixed-size` with the same defaults (`tokens=400`, `overlap=80`).

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[memory file] -> chunkMarkdown(fixedSize) -> [MemoryChunk[]] -> embed + index

After:
[memory file] -> resolveChunkingStrategy(config) -> strategy.chunk(content) -> [MemoryChunk[]] -> embed + index
                        |
                        ├── fixed-size    (token window + overlap)
                        ├── markdown-heading (heading boundaries + fixed-size fallback)
                        ├── sentence      (sentence boundaries + token budget)
                        ├── semantic      (embedding cosine distance breakpoints)
                        ├── lumber        (LLM paragraph content-shift detection)
                        └── hichunk       (hierarchical multi-level LLM segmentation)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` (semantic/lumber/hichunk use existing embedding provider and completion model infrastructure)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / Bun
- Model/provider: Any embedding provider (OpenAI, local, etc.) + any completion model for lumber/hichunk
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```json
  {
    "models": {
      "mode": "merge",
      "providers": {
        "sglang": {
          "baseUrl": "***",
          "apiKey": "***",
          "api": "openai-completions",
          "models": [
            {
              "id": "deepseek-v3.2",
              "name": "deepseek-v3.2",
              "reasoning": false,
              "input": [
                "text"
              ]
            }
          ]
        },
        "vllm": {
          "baseUrl": "***",
          "apiKey": "***",
          "api": "openai-completions",
          "models": [
            {
              "id": "Youtu-HiChunk",
              "name": "Youtu-HiChunk",
              "reasoning": false,
              "input": [
                "text"
              ]
            }
          ]
        }
      }
    },
    "agents": {
      "defaults": {
        "model": {
          "primary": "sglang/deepseek-v3.2"
        },
        "models": {
          "sglang/deepseek-v3.2": {},
          "vllm/Youtu-HiChunk": {}
        },
        "workspace": "/root/.openclaw/workspace",
        "memorySearch": {
          "enabled": true,
          "provider": "auto",
          "remote": {
            "baseUrl": "***",       // embeding model base url
            "apiKey": "***",        // embeding model api key
            "batch": {
              "enabled": true
            }
          },
          "model": "youtu-embedding-2B",
          "chunking": {
            "strategy": "markdown-heading",     // default "fixed-size"
            "tokens": 400,
            "overlap": 80,
            "maxTokens": 400,
            "maxDepth": 3,
            "targetTokens": 400,
            "overlapSentences": 1,
            "bufferSize": 1,
            "breakpointPercentileThreshold": 95,
            "theta": 550,
            "completionModel": "vllm/Youtu-HiChunk",    // set to "sglang/deepseek-v3.2" for lumber chunking strategy, and "vllm/Youtu-HiChunk" for hichunk strategy.
            "windowSize": 16384,
            "lineMaxLen": 100,
            "maxLevel": 2,
            "recurrentType": 2,
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Set `agents.defaults.memorySearch.chunking.strategy` to any of the 6 strategies (e.g. `"markdown-heading"`).
2. Add or update memory files in the workspace.
3. Run `openclaw memory index --force` or trigger memory indexing.

### Expected

- Memory files are chunked using the selected strategy.
- Chunks have correct `startLine`/`endLine` ranges.
- Changing strategy or strategy params triggers a full reindex on next sync.
- Omitting `chunking.strategy` uses `fixed-size` with original defaults (backward-compat).

### Actual

- (Verified as expected)

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

`chunking.test.ts` — 50+ test cases across all strategies and the factory pass. `manager-reindex-state.test.ts` — reindex detection tests pass for all strategy drift scenarios and legacy compat.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - All 6 strategies produce valid non-empty chunks for representative documents.
  - `fixed-size` output matches pre-refactor `chunkMarkdown` output (identical chunks).
  - `markdown-heading` correctly splits at h1/h2/h3 and sub-chunks oversized sections.
  - `sentence` respects token budget and overlap carry.
  - `semantic` calls `embedBatch` and splits on high cosine distance.
  - `lumber` and `hichunk` call the completion function and handle LLM errors gracefully.
  - Config resolution applies defaults and clamping per-strategy.
  - Reindex detection triggers on strategy change, param change, and legacy meta migration.
  - End-to-end sync + search with each strategy against a real SQLite index.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Empty content → `[]` for all strategies.
  - Single sentence/paragraph → single chunk (short-circuit path).
  - CJK-heavy text in fixed-size and sentence strategies.
  - Missing `completionModel` for lumber/hichunk → clear error message.
  - Missing embedding provider for `semantic` → clear error message.
  - Legacy meta without `chunkingConfig.strategy` → correct backward-compat comparison.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Performance impact of semantic strategy with large embedding batches.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — default strategy remains `fixed-size` with unchanged defaults; existing configs and index meta are fully supported.
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — new optional config keys under `agents.defaults.memorySearch.chunking.*`.
- Migration needed? `No` — existing indexes continue to work. Changing strategy triggers auto-reindex on next sync.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: `N/A`

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `lumber` and `hichunk` strategies make LLM calls during indexing, which can be slow and costly for large memory directories.
  - Mitigation: These are opt-in strategies, not defaults. Both strategies handle LLM errors gracefully with fallback chunking. Token estimation uses local CJK-aware heuristics to minimize unnecessary LLM calls.
- Risk: Semantic strategy requires a working embedding provider at index time; provider outage would block indexing.
  - Mitigation: Factory throws a clear error if provider is missing. Users can fall back to non-provider strategies.
- Risk: Reindex detection for new strategies may produce false negatives on very old index meta formats.
  - Mitigation: `chunkingConfigDiffers` includes explicit legacy path that triggers reindex when switching from legacy meta to any non-fixed-size strategy.

target.title: fix(slack): close WebSocket connections on provider stop to prevent leak
target.body:
## Summary

- **Root cause**: When the gateway health monitor detects stale Slack sockets and restarts providers, `@slack/bolt`'s `app.stop()` does not fully tear down the underlying `SocketModeClient` WebSocket. With multi-account Slack providers (5 accounts in our case) being restarted repeatedly, leaked connections accumulate — growing memory to ~2.5GB and causing OOM crashes.
- **Fix**: Explicitly call `SocketModeClient.disconnect()` before `app.stop()` on every cleanup path, and add a `gateway.stopAccount` hook so the gateway no longer fast-path skips Slack providers during shutdown.
- Mirrors the approach from `fix(feishu): close WebSocket connections on monitor stop` (#52844).

### Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/reconnect-policy.ts` | Extract `getSocketModeClient()`, add `disconnectSocketModeClient()` that calls `SocketModeClient.disconnect()` |
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/provider.ts` | Call `disconnectSocketModeClient()` inside `gracefulStopSlackApp()` before `app.stop()` |
| `extensions/slack/src/channel.ts` | Add `gateway.stopAccount` hook so `stopChannel()` runs the full abort→cleanup→await lifecycle |

### Evidence from production

- **269 WebSocket connection IDs** observed in a single day's logs (expected: ~5 at any time)
- **71 provider start events** (5 accounts × ~14 restart cycles)
- **21 stale-socket restarts** triggered by health monitor
- **2 OOM crashes** (at 00:22 and 10:56) + multiple near-OOM restarts, all peaking at ~2.5GB
- Gateway process consuming **49% CPU and 1.8GB RAM** within 14 minutes of restart

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test:extension slack` — provider reconnect, auth-error, interop, and channel tests pass (pre-existing failures from missing `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` dep are unrelated)
- [x] Lint passes (oxlint, 0 errors on changed files)
- [x] Config workaround (`channelStaleEventThresholdMinutes: 360`) applied in parallel — fresh gateway instance stable at 468MB vs 1.8GB before
- [ ] Verify on production instance after upgrade

## AI-assisted PR

- [x] Mark as AI-assisted in the PR title or description
- [x] Note the degree of testing: fully tested — `pnpm test:extension slack` passes, lint clean, production evidence collected
- [x] Confirm we understand what the code does: the fix adds explicit `SocketModeClient.disconnect()` calls before `app.stop()` to ensure the underlying WebSocket is torn down, preventing connection accumulation across health-monitor restart cycles
- [x] Codex review run and findings addressed (removed unnecessary `stopAccount` hook per Codex feedback in b1506e2)
- [x] Bot review conversations resolved (Greptile lint suppression + Codex stop hook — both addressed and resolved)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: Allow configuring gateway Permissions-Policy
target.body:
## Summary
- add `gateway.http.securityHeaders.permissionsPolicy` as a supported runtime config
- change the default gateway `Permissions-Policy` to block camera and geolocation without blocking microphone
- thread the new header through runtime config, schema metadata, and gateway HTTP handling
- add focused tests for default, override, and explicit disable behavior

## Why
Talk mode in Control UI needs microphone access, but the current default gateway header hard-blocks it for every response:

```http
Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=()
```

That makes microphone-based UX regress after updates unless the installed bundle is patched locally. This change moves the fix into source and makes the behavior configurable in the same place as other gateway security headers.

## Validation
- `corepack pnpm config:schema:gen`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/http-common.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-config.test.ts`

## Notes
A full repo-wide pre-commit run hit an unrelated existing failure under `extensions/feishu`, so I validated the affected gateway path directly.

target.title: Adding OpenRouter support for allowedModels
target.body:
🤖 AI-assisted PR, validated by a human (me) — tested locally, all openrouter extension tests pass.

  ## Background

I'm the founder of [ClawOps](https://clawops.io), a managed AI assistant platform built on top of OpenClaw. While there are other hosting options for OpenClaw, we're not just a VPS provider: our goal is to give non-technical users immediate, zero-config access to personal AI agents. Let's face it, for technical users it's easy enough to set up and configure OpenClaw agents, but there is still loads of config tweaking behind the scenes to optimise memory, capability and cost. We want to take that out of the equation.

We believe personal (and business) agents will become as common as Netflix subscriptions.

One of our core differentiators is [Model Stacks](https://docs.clawops.io/features/model-stacks/). The idea is users operate on cost effective LLM stacks (e.g. Big Tech, Indie, Premium), letting users get intelligent routing without needing to understand the underlying model landscape.

In addition we have [Smart Switching](https://docs.clawops.io/features/smart-switching/), which automatically changes the stack based on the user's credits. This means that even when they run low on credits the agent stays on and responsive using free models.

OpenRouter already supports automatic model selection for cost optimisation, see [Using Auto Model for Cost Optimization](https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/coding-agents/openclaw-integration#using-auto-model-for-cost-optimization). OpenRouter also supports further config to use `auto` with constraints to specific models via the [`plugins` API](https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/routing/routers/auto-router). However, this config is not currently supported in OpenClaw, this PR aims to solve that.

  ---

  ## Summary

  Config example (exact model IDs):
 ```yaml
  model: openrouter/openrouter/auto
  params:
    autoRouter:
      allowedModels:
        - anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5
        - google/gemini-2.5-flash
        - openai/gpt-5-nano
```

  Config example (wildcard patterns):
 ```yaml
  model: openrouter/openrouter/auto
  params:
    autoRouter:
      allowedModels:
        - anthropic/*
        - google/*
```

  - Problem: openrouter/openrouter/auto routes across all OpenRouter models with no way to constrain the pool, making it unsuitable for cost-controlled or curated deployments.
  - Why it matters: Users running fixed model stacks (e.g. Big Tech / Indie / Premium tier pools) want intelligent per-prompt routing within their stack, not across the entire marketplace.
  - What changed: Added injectAutoRouterPlugin() in extensions/openrouter/index.ts that injects plugins: [{ id: "auto-router", allowed_models: [...] }] into the request payload when params.autoRouter.allowedModels is set. 3 new unit tests added. Docs updated.
  - What did NOT change: No core files touched. No type schema changes (params is already Record<string, unknown>). No behavior change when autoRouter is absent — existing configs unaffected.

 ###  Change Type

  - Feature 
  - Docs

  ###  Scope 

  - Integrations

  ### Linked Issue/PR

  None

 ### User-visible / Behavior Changes

 New optional config param params.autoRouter.allowedModels on the openrouter/openrouter/auto model. Accepts exact model IDs (anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5) or wildcard patterns (anthropic/*). When set, auto-routing is constrained to the provided model list. When absent, behavior is identical to current, no change to defaults.

  ### Security Impact 

  - New permissions/capabilities? No
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
  - New/changed network calls? No — same OpenRouter endpoint, additional field in existing payload
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? No
  - Data access scope changed? No

 ### Repro + Verification

  Environment

  - OS: Windows 11 (WSL2)
  - Runtime/container: Node.js (pnpm)
  - Model/provider: openrouter/openrouter/auto via OpenRouter API
  - Integration/channel: Direct API / Telegram
  - Relevant config (redacted): see config examples above

 ### Steps

  1. Set model to openrouter/openrouter/auto with params.autoRouter.allowedModels: ["google/gemini-2.5-flash"]
  2. Send a message through the gateway
  3. Check OpenRouter Activity dashboard

  Expected

  - Only google/gemini-2.5-flash appears in the Activity log (single-model list = deterministic)

  Actual

  - Confirmed — only the allowlisted model was selected

  Evidence

  - Failing test/log before + passing after — 3 new unit tests in pi-embedded-runner-extraparams.test.ts, all passing

 ### Human Verification

  - Verified scenarios: allowedModels set with one model (deterministic), allowedModels absent (no plugin injected), allowedModels: [] empty array (no plugin injected)
  - Edge cases checked: Empty array treated same as absent — no plugins key injected. Existing provider routing param unaffected.
  - What you did not verify: Multi-model list behavior beyond unit test level (OpenRouter's internal selection logic is a black box); behavior with streaming edge cases at high concurrency.

   ### Compatibility / Migration

  - Backward compatible? Yes
  - Config/env changes? No
  - Migration needed? No

  ### Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

  - How to disable/revert quickly: Remove autoRouter.allowedModels from config — feature is opt-in, no effect when absent
  - Files/config to restore: extensions/openrouter/index.ts (revert injectAutoRouterPlugin addition and wrapStreamFn update)
  - Known bad symptoms: If plugins injection causes unexpected model selection, check OpenRouter Activity dashboard — the allowed_models field will be
  visible in the request log

  ### Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: OpenRouter silently ignores plugins field on non-auto models, leading to no error but also no constraint.
  - Mitigation: Feature is gated on autoRouter.allowedModels being present — only applies when explicitly configured. Users setting this on a non-auto
  model is a config error, not a safety risk.

target.title: fix: validate hooks.token requirement at config.patch time

## Problem

When `hooks.enabled: true` is set via `config.patch` without `hooks.token`, the gateway fails on startup with an unclear error message (`hooks.enabled requires hooks.token`). Users have no guidance on how to fix it.

## Fix

1. **Config-time validation**: Added a cross-field validation check in `validateConfigObjectWithPluginsBase` that catches `hooks.enabled=true` without `hooks.token` and returns a clear error at `config.patch` time — before the gateway restarts.

2. **Improved startup error**: Updated the error message in `resolveHooksConfig` to suggest the exact `config.patch` command needed to set the token.

## Changes

- `src/config/validation.ts`: Added cross-field validation for `hooks.enabled` requiring `hooks.token`
- `src/gateway/hooks.ts`: Improved error message with actionable guidance

target.title: fix(usage): attribute per-channel stats by origin provider, not delivery channel
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #52436

The `/usage` dashboard incorrectly attributes per-channel token usage. When a session crosses channel boundaries (e.g., a webchat-originated message delivered into a Telegram group), the usage is bucketed under the **delivery channel** instead of the **originating channel**, causing webchat and Telegram stats to appear swapped.

**Root cause:** `src/gateway/server-methods/usage.ts:610-611` resolves the channel via:

```typescript
const channel = merged.storeEntry?.channel ?? merged.storeEntry?.origin?.provider;
```

`storeEntry.channel` is set by `deriveGroupSessionPatch()` and reflects the delivery/group channel. `origin.provider` reflects the originating channel. The nullish coalescing priority is inverted — `origin.provider` should take precedence for usage attribution.

**Fix:** Invert the priority on both `channel` (line 610) and `chatType` (line 611) so `origin.provider`/`origin.chatType` are preferred over `storeEntry.channel`/`storeEntry.chatType`. Total usage is unaffected; only per-channel bucketing changes.

## Changes

- `src/gateway/server-methods/usage.ts`: 2-line fix inverting the nullish coalescing order
- `src/gateway/server-methods/usage.sessions-usage.test.ts`: Regression test seeding two cross-channel sessions and verifying per-channel attribution uses the origin provider

## Test plan

- [x] New regression test: seeds two sessions with swapped `origin.provider` vs `storeEntry.channel`, verifies `aggregates.byChannel` and per-session `channel`/`chatType` reflect the origin
- [x] All existing usage tests pass (`usage.test.ts`, `usage.sessions-usage.test.ts`, `usage-aggregates.test.ts`)

target.title: fix(doctor): skip cleanup hints for active gateway and downgrade exec SecretRef errors to warnings
target.body:
## Fixes #52510

### Problem

`openclaw doctor` has two false-positive issues:

1. **Cleanup hints suggest removing the active gateway** - When doctor detects ANY extra gateway-like services (e.g., an unrelated `imessage-watcher`), it unconditionally generates cleanup commands for the main gateway plist. Following these hints kills the running gateway and causes downtime.

2. **SecretRef exit code 1** - Doctor exits with code 1 when encountering `exec:keychain:` SecretRef values in config. These are resolved at gateway runtime and are expected to be unavailable during doctor execution.

### Changes

**Bug 1: `src/daemon/inspect.ts` + `src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.ts`**
- `renderGatewayServiceCleanupHints()` now takes the actual detected extra services as input
- Generates per-service cleanup commands based on what was actually found
- Returns empty array when no services are provided
- Call site passes only non-legacy extra services

**Bug 2: `src/config/types.secrets.ts` + `src/commands/doctor.ts`**
- Doctor sets `OPENCLAW_ALLOW_EXEC_SECRETREF_FAILURES=true` during execution (with proper try/finally cleanup)
- `assertSecretInputResolved()` detects doctor context and downgrades `exec:` SecretRef failures to warnings instead of throwing
- Original throwing behavior preserved for all non-doctor contexts

### Testing

- Existing tests use mocked `renderGatewayServiceCleanupHints`, so signature change is safe
- Manually verified: doctor no longer suggests removing active gateway when only non-gateway extras are detected
- Manually verified: doctor completes with exit 0 when `exec:keychain:` SecretRefs are present

target.title: fix(acp): treat missing cwd as stale bound session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: bound ACP sessions stayed attached after the runtime `cwd` disappeared, so retries kept failing against a dead session.
- Why it matters: affected threads remain wedged until someone manually clears the stale binding.
- What changed: treat missing-`cwd` `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` errors as stale-session signals in both dispatch-time cleanup and reset-in-place cleanup, and cover both paths with regressions.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): stale cleanup still only triggers for `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`; this does not broaden cleanup to generic turn failures.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #60720
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: stale-session detection only recognized metadata and resource-not-found init failures, so a missing ACP runtime working directory did not trigger stale unbind.
- Missing detection / guardrail: reset-in-place failure handling returned the error without clearing the stale binding, and no regression covered missing-`cwd` init failures.
- Contributing context (if known): the old fix branch had drifted too far from current `main`, but the bug still reproduced on April 18, 2026.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test

target.title: fix(feishu): treat OpenChat (oc_) topic groups as group chats
target.body:
## Summary

Feishu 话题群 (OpenChat / topic groups) bot-receive events carry `chat_type: "p2p"` even though the `chat_id` starts with `oc_` — Feishu's OpenChat/topic-group prefix. The existing `isGroup` guard in `bot.ts`:

```ts
const isGroup = ctx.chatType === 'group';
```

therefore classified every topic-group message as a DM, routing it to the main agent session instead of the configured channel session. Replies never appeared inside the 话题群. (Reported log: `received message from ou_... in oc_ceaacf99d602be4842fe4505a77a4e70 (p2p)`)

The same misclassification also affected `isMentionForwardRequest` in `mention.ts`.

## Changes

- **`extensions/feishu/src/chat-type.ts`** (new) — `isFeishuGroupChat(chatType, chatId)` helper extracted into its own module to avoid circular imports (`bot.ts` already imports from `mention.ts` and vice-versa).
  - Returns `true` when `chat_type === 'group'` OR when `chat_type === 'p2p'` and `chat_id.startsWith('oc_')`.
- **`extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts`** — `isGroup` now uses `isFeishuGroupChat`.
- **`extensions/feishu/src/mention.ts`** — `isDirectMessage` now uses `isFeishuGroupChat`.
- **`extensions/feishu/src/chat-type.test.ts`** (new) — 4 unit tests covering: normal group, real DM (`p2p_` prefix), OpenChat topic group (`oc_` prefix), and `private` chat type.

## Test

```
pnpm vitest run extensions/feishu/src/chat-type.test.ts
✓ extensions/feishu/src/chat-type.test.ts (4 tests) 3ms
Test Files: 1 passed (1)
     Tests: 4 passed (4)
```

Closes #52238

target.title: fix(feishu): prevent duplicate text send when sendMedia has no media URL
target.body:
## Summary

- In the Feishu `sendMedia` handler, text is sent unconditionally at line 160 before checking for `mediaUrl`
- When `sendMedia` is called with text but no media URL, the text-only fallback at line 196 sends the same text again
- This results in duplicate messages in Feishu chats

## Root cause

The `sendMedia` function has three code paths:

1. **Text + media**: sends text (line 161), then sends media (line 173) — correct
2. **Media only**: skips text (line 160), sends media (line 173) — correct
3. **Text only (no media)**: sends text (line 161), skips media (line 171), sends text AGAIN (line 196) — **bug**

The pre-media text send at line 160 was not guarded by a `mediaUrl` check, so it fires even when there is no media to follow.

## Fix

Add `&& mediaUrl` to the condition at line 160, so the companion text message is only sent when media follows it. The text-only fallback at line 196 handles the no-media case correctly on its own.

## Test plan

- [x] All 13 Feishu outbound tests pass (`pnpm test -- extensions/feishu/src/outbound`)
- [x] Code inspection confirms the three code paths now produce exactly one text send each

target.title: feat(docker): add optional uv installation for skills and extensions
target.body:
## Summary
Add `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_UV` build arg to the Dockerfile to optionally install [uv](https://astral.sh/uv), a fast Python package manager, during image build.
## Motivation
Several OpenClaw workspace skills (e.g. `stock-analysis`) require uv for managing Python dependencies. In Docker deployments, manually installing uv inside a running container causes it to be lost on restart. This change allows uv to be baked into the image at build time, making it persistent across container restarts.
## Implementation
- New `ARG OPENCLAW_INSTALL_UV=""` build flag (default: off)
- When enabled, installs uv to `/usr/local/bin` so it is accessible to the non-root `node` user
- Uses the officialAstral install script: `curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh`
## Build example
```bash
docker build --build-arg OPENCLAW_INSTALL_UV=1 -t openclaw:uv .

target.title: fix: chat.abort RPC now stops embedded Pi agent, sub-agents, and session queues
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #38277

The `chat.abort` RPC handler only aborted HTTP streaming connections but did not stop the embedded Pi agent run, spawned sub-agents, or clear follow-up message queues. Clicking **Stop** in the webchat UI left the agent running and consuming tokens, unlike the `/stop` text command.

## Changes

- Added `abortEmbeddedPiRun(sessionId)`, `clearSessionQueues()`, and `stopSubagentsForRequester()` calls to both abort paths in `chat.abort` (by sessionKey and by runId)
- Both paths are guarded by `res.aborted` to avoid unnecessary work
- Added test file `chat.abort-embedded-run.test.ts` with 3 tests covering both paths + negative case

## How it was tested

- All 11 `chat.abort*` tests pass (3 new + 8 existing)
- Fix mirrors the existing correct implementation in `tryFastAbortFromMessage()` (used by `/stop`)


> This PR was developed with AI assistance.

target.title: fix: persist pending followup queues across gateway restart
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #51620 — gateway restart drops queued and in-flight inbound messages.

## Changes

### New: `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/persist.ts`
Serializes all non-empty `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` entries to `state/pending-messages.json` before shutdown. Strips non-portable fields (`config`, `skillsSnapshot`) and stores a version marker + timestamp.

### New: `src/gateway/server-pending-messages.ts`
On startup, reads the persisted file, injects missed messages as system events into their respective sessions (so the agent processes them on next turn), then deletes the file. Includes a 5-minute staleness guard to avoid replaying ancient messages from stale files.

### Modified: `src/gateway/server-close.ts`
Calls `persistFollowupQueues(FOLLOWUP_QUEUES)` before `chatRunState.clear()` so queue state is captured while still available. Best-effort — failure doesn't block shutdown.

### Modified: `src/gateway/server-startup.ts`
Calls `replayPersistedPendingMessages()` 1.5s after startup (after channels initialize), alongside the existing `scheduleRestartSentinelWake`.

## Tests

16 new tests covering:
- `persistFollowupQueues`: empty queues, non-empty queues, config stripping, multiple queues
- `consumePersistedQueues`: no file, read+delete, staleness guard (>5min), corrupt file, wrong version
- Round-trip: persist → consume
- `replayPersistedPendingMessages`: no entries, empty entries, system event injection, multiple queues, prompt truncation, empty items skip

## Limitations / Follow-up

This PR covers **Phase 1**: persisting in-memory followup queue items across restart.

Still not covered (follow-up work):
- **Phase 2**: Messages arriving during the drain window (hitting `GatewayDrainingError`) — these are rejected at the channel level before reaching the queue
- **Phase 3**: Channel-level message acknowledgment deferral — for channels that support it (Slack Socket Mode), defer ACK until message is processed or persisted so the platform can redeliver

## Testing

```
npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/queue/persist.test.ts src/gateway/server-pending-messages.test.ts
```

All 16 tests pass.

---

Upstream issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/51620

target.title: fix(sandbox): fall back to static channel registry for sandbox explain channel resolution
target.body:
Fixes #51245

## Problem

`openclaw sandbox explain --session telegram:slash:12345` shows `channel: (unknown)` for Telegram slash sessions, causing elevated `allowFrom` checks to fail even when the Telegram sender is correctly allowlisted.

## Root Cause

`inferProviderFromSessionKey()` in `sandbox-explain.ts` uses `normalizeAnyChannelId()` which depends on the **runtime channel plugin registry**. When running from the CLI (`sandbox explain`), channel plugins may not be loaded, so built-in channels like `telegram` don't resolve.

## Fix

Fall back to the static `normalizeChatChannelId()` which uses the hardcoded `CHAT_CHANNEL_META` registry and always resolves built-in channels like `telegram`, `discord`, `slack`, etc.

## Test

```
pnpm test -- src/commands/sandbox-explain.test.ts
✓ 1 test passed
```

target.title: whatsapp: Allow different ack emoji for different agents
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `resolveAckReaction` in `src/agents/identity.ts` cast `channelCfg?.ackReaction` directly to `string` at L1/L2. WhatsApp's `ackReaction` is an object (`{ emoji?, direct?, group? }`), not a plain string — this would throw `channelReaction.trim is not a function` at runtime if called for WhatsApp. The WhatsApp extension therefore bypassed `resolveAckReaction` entirely, reading `ackConfig?.emoji` directly and losing the agent identity emoji fallback (L4).
- **Why it matters:** Multi-agent WhatsApp setups (e.g. two agents sharing one number via `bindings`) had no way to give each agent a distinct ack reaction emoji — the channel-level emoji was always used regardless of which agent was responding.
- **What changed:** Added `extractAckEmoji()` helper to handle both plain-string and object-with-emoji formats in L1/L2; exported `resolveAckReaction` from `channel-feedback.ts`; WhatsApp extension now uses `resolveAckReaction` so the agent identity emoji serves as a per-agent fallback.
- **What did NOT change:** Users with no `ackReaction` config continue to get no reaction (the `ackConfig !== undefined` guard preserves existing behaviour exactly).

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users with `channels.whatsapp.ackReaction` configured but no `emoji` field (e.g. `{ direct: true, group: "mentions" }`) will now see the responding agent's identity emoji used as the ack reaction instead of no reaction. Users with an explicit `emoji` value or no `ackReaction` config at all are unaffected.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22
- Integration/channel: WhatsApp

### Steps

1. Configure two agents with different identity emojis and a WhatsApp binding routing each agent to different senders
2. Remove `emoji` from `channels.whatsapp.ackReaction` (keep `direct`/`group` settings)
3. Send a WhatsApp message from each sender

### Expected

- Each agent ACKs with its own identity emoji

### Actual (before fix)

- No ack reaction sent (emoji resolved to empty string)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

4 new tests added to `src/agents/identity.test.ts` covering WhatsApp object-format at channel level, agent identity fallback when no emoji field, empty-emoji disable, and account-level object format. All 10 tests pass.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: existing `ackReaction: { emoji: "🍓" }` config unchanged; no config = no reaction unchanged; object without emoji = agent identity emoji used
- Edge cases checked: empty string emoji still disables reactions; account-level object format resolved correctly
- What you did not verify: live WhatsApp send (tested via unit tests and config inspection)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No — zero config changes needed to update
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/ack-reaction.ts` to read `ackConfig?.emoji` directly
- Files/config to restore: none required
- Known bad symptoms: unexpected ack reactions appearing for users with partial `ackReaction` config (no `emoji` field set)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: users with `ackReaction: {}` or `ackReaction: { direct: false }` (no emoji) start getting reactions from the agent identity emoji
  - Mitigation: this is an edge-case config (most users either set emoji or omit ackReaction entirely); the change is intentional and documented in User-visible Changes above

target.title: fix(onboard): discover model context window from proxy /model/info API
target.body:
## Summary

- During onboard, copilot-proxy and LiteLLM providers hardcode `contextWindow: 128000` and `maxTokens: 8192` for all discovered models
- This causes premature context compaction for models with larger windows (e.g. `claude-opus-4.6-1m` routed through LiteLLM with 1M context), wasting ~87% of available capacity
- Fix: query the proxy /v1/model/info endpoint during setup to read actual max_input_tokens and max_output_tokens per model, falling back to previous defaults when the endpoint is unavailable

## Root Cause

LiteLLM exposes model capabilities via model_info in its config (e.g. max_input_tokens: 1000000 for Opus 4.6 1M), and serves this data at /v1/model/info. However, the onboard flow never queries this endpoint - it assigns the same 128k/8192 defaults to every model regardless of actual capability.

This means the context window guard triggers compaction at ~100k tokens even when the underlying model supports 1M, leading to unnecessary history truncation in long sessions.

## Changes

- **extensions/copilot-proxy/index.ts**: add fetchModelInfoMap() that queries /v1/model/info during onboard and passes discovered contextWindow/maxTokens to buildModelDefinition()
- **src/commands/onboard-auth.config-litellm.ts**: add fetchLitellmModelInfo() export and accept optional modelInfoOverrides in config appliers (keeps functions sync for type compatibility)
- **src/commands/auth-choice.apply.api-key-providers.ts**: call fetchLitellmModelInfo() before applying config, passing real values via closure

## Backward Compatibility

- When /v1/model/info is unavailable (proxy not running, non-LiteLLM proxy, network error), falls back silently to previous 128k/8192 defaults
- applyLitellmConfig and applyLitellmProviderConfig remain sync - the async fetch happens in the caller
- Non-interactive onboard path continues using defaults (LiteLLM may not be running during non-interactive setup)

## Test plan

- [x] pnpm tsgo passes (type check)
- [x] onboard-auth.config-shared.test.ts - 6/6 pass
- [x] onboard-auth.test.ts - 31/31 pass
- [x] onboard-custom.test.ts - 29/29 pass
- [x] Verified locally: with LiteLLM running, opus model gets contextWindow: 1000000 instead of 128000
- [x] Verified locally: with LiteLLM stopped, falls back to 128000 default gracefully

target.title: Fix embedded read-image media fallback
target.body:
## Summary
- fall back to the originating tool args when embedded tool results contain image blocks but no `details.path`
- thread the original `tool_execution_start` args into the non-verbose media emission path
- add regression tests for direct extraction and `handleToolExecutionEnd` media delivery

## Why
Issue [#41744](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/41744) has converged on the same failure mode across multiple reports:
- `toolResult` contains `type: "image"`
- `toolResult.details.path` is sometimes missing
- the runtime therefore drops the image before it reaches outbound `mediaUrls`

This is especially visible for embedded `read` calls that successfully load an image file but only preserve the original file path in the start args (`path` / `file_path`).

## Approach
When `extractToolResultMediaPaths(...)` sees image content with no `MEDIA:` token and no `details.path`, it now optionally falls back to the original tool args:
- `path`
- `file_path`
- `filePath`

`handleToolExecutionEnd(...)` already has access to `startData?.args`, so the non-verbose media emission path now passes those args through.

## Testing
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.tools.media.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.media.test.ts`

target.title: Telegram: allow native channel commands in explicitly allowed chats
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Telegram `channel_post` updates could carry regular text, but native slash commands such as `/status` were not authorized
correctly in channel relay setups.
- Why it matters: OpenClaw bot-to-bot Telegram channel workflows could not use native commands even when the chat itself was explicitly
allowed.
- What changed: Native Telegram commands now accept `channel_post` inputs and authorize them by explicit chat allow status for the
receiving account.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not redefine sender-scoped `groups[chatId].allowFrom` semantics for `channel_post`,
because Telegram does not expose the originating human sender on that surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #49062

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Telegram native commands such as `/status` now work from explicitly allowed `channel_post` chats.
- Disallowed Telegram channel chats still receive `"This group is not allowed."`
- Regular group/DM sender-based command authorization behavior is unchanged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

This PR enables Telegram native command execution from explicitly allowed channel chats, which is a command execution surface change. The
risk is bounded by existing explicit chat allow checks. Disallowed chats remain blocked, and regression tests cover both allowed and denied
channel cases as well as `commands.allowFrom.telegram` precedence.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

OpenClaw version: 2026.3.13
Operating system: all OS
Install method: npm global
Model: gpt-5.4
Provider / routing chain: chatgpt oauth



### Steps

1. Configure a Telegram account with the target channel explicitly allowed.
2. Send /status as a channel_post in that Telegram channel.
3. Observe native command authorization and reply behavior.

### Expected

- Explicitly allowed channel chats can execute native commands such as /status.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the command was rejected or dropped on channel_post.
- After this fix, the command is authorized by explicit chat allow and executes normally.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
    - /status from an explicitly allowed Telegram channel now succeeds.
    - Disallowed channel chats remain blocked by chat allowlist checks.
- Edge cases checked:
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not block explicitly allowed channel_post commands.
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not bypass denied channel_post chat allowlists.
- What you did not verify:
    - Sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom semantics for channel_post
    - Live plugin command behavior on Telegram channels
    - Webhook-specific Telegram deployment paths

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for
maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
    - Revert this PR, or disable Telegram native commands temporarily with channels.telegram.commands.native: false
- Files/config to restore:
    - extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
    - Explicitly allowed Telegram channel /status still returns "You are not authorized to use this command."
    - Disallowed channel chats start executing native commands unexpectedly

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write None.

- Risk:
    - channel_post authorization now uses explicit chat allow rather than sender-scoped identity.
    - Mitigation:
        - This is limited to Telegram channel_post, where Telegram does not expose the originating human sender.
        - Explicit chat allow is still required.
        - Regression tests cover both allow and deny behavior.
- Risk:
    - Future contributors may assume sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom also applies to channel_post.
    - Mitigation:
        - Scope is explicitly documented in this PR.
        - A separate follow-up issue will track channel_post sender-scoped allowlist semantics.

target.title: fix(ios): picker adaptive onChange loop + OSLog string concatenation
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the iOS chat UI had two SwiftUI/runtime stability issues in the current patchset: the adaptive thinking picker could loop when selection state did not match tags, and streaming scroll updates could fire during the same frame/layout pass.
- Why it matters: users can see selection churn, repeated per-frame update warnings, and unstable chat-scroll behavior during rapid streaming.
- What changed: aligned the picker with the adaptive value and deferred the streaming scroll update past the current layout pass.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway protocol changes, no model/runtime changes outside the shared chat UI, and no non-iOS platform behavior changes.
- AI-assisted: yes (AI-assisted implementation, contributor-reviewed).
- Testing level: locally built and manually verified on iOS.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: SwiftUI selection/layout behavior was being driven with state that did not fully align to the rendered picker tags and with streaming scroll updates occurring during the active frame/layout cycle.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no focused UI/runtime validation covering the adaptive picker state plus rapid streaming-update scroll behavior.

target.title: fix(tui): strip tagged thinking from string content
target.body:
Fixes #40736

## Summary

- strip inline `<think>...</think>` reasoning tags from TUI string content when thinking display is off
- surface tagged reasoning separately in TUI when thinking display is on
- add regression coverage for tagged-string assistant payloads in `tui-formatters`

## Root cause

TUI already handled structured reasoning blocks when assistant messages arrived as `content: [{ type: "thinking" }, { type: "text" }]`.

But some providers send reasoning inline inside plain string content using `<think>...</think>` tags. In that path, `extractContentFromMessage()` treated the whole string as visible text, so the raw reasoning leaked into the TUI even when `showThinking` was disabled.

## Testing

- `PATH=/opt/homebrew/opt/node@23/bin:$PATH pnpm --dir /Users/zhangxuanyang/Desktop/DDDDAO/openclaw/worktrees/fix-tui-thinking-tag-leak exec vitest run src/tui/tui-formatters.test.ts src/tui/tui-stream-assembler.test.ts src/shared/text/reasoning-tags.test.ts`

target.title: feat(telegram): add /settings inline keyboard control panel
target.body:
## Problem

Configuring Telegram access control today means editing `openclaw.json` by hand or knowing exact `/config set channels.telegram.dmPolicy open` paths. The settings that matter most — who can DM the bot, which groups are open, whether unknown senders get paired or blocked — are the hardest to discover and the easiest to get wrong.

1. **Access control is invisible** — `dmPolicy`, `groupPolicy`, and `streaming` are the three settings users change most, but there is no in-app surface that shows what they are set to, what the valid options are, or what each option does. A user who wants to switch from pairing mode to an open DM policy has to know the exact config path, the exact string value, and the fact that the change takes effect without a restart.
2. **Errors are silent and destructive** — a typo in a config path silently does nothing; a typo in a value can crash the gateway and kill all channels (see #25007). Users shouldn't need to hand-edit JSON to change who can talk to their bot.
3. **No feedback loop** — after running `/config set`, there's no confirmation of what changed or what the current state is. Users have to run `/config get` separately and parse raw JSON output to verify.

The `/model` command already solved this for model selection with inline keyboard buttons (`model-buttons.ts`). This PR extends the same pattern to the access control and streaming settings that users actually need to configure day-to-day.

## Solution

A `/settings` native command that renders an inline keyboard control panel in Telegram DMs. Users tap buttons to navigate submenus and toggle settings — no config paths to memorize, no JSON to edit.

### Main menu
```
Settings

DM Policy: pairing
Group Policy: open
Streaming: partial

[DM Policy]  [Group Policy]
[Streaming]  [Allowlist]
```

### Submenu (e.g. DM Policy)
```
DM Policy

pairing ✓ — Unknown senders get a pairing code
allowlist — Only allow senders in allowFrom
open — Allow all inbound DMs
disabled — Ignore all inbound DMs

[pairing ✓]
[allowlist]
[open]
[disabled]
[<< Back]
```

Tapping a value applies it immediately and re-renders the submenu with the checkmark moved to the new selection. The allowlist view is read-only (shows current entries, directs to `/allowlist` for mutations).

## Implementation

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `src/telegram/settings-buttons.ts` (223 LOC) | Pure functions: callback data parsing (`cfg_` prefix, all under 64 bytes), keyboard builders for main menu, submenus with checkmarks, and read-only allowlist view |
| `src/telegram/settings-panel.ts` (175 LOC) | Panel logic: command response builder, callback handler, config mutation via async snapshot pipeline (`readConfigFileSnapshotForWrite` → clone `resolved` → `setConfigValueAtPath` → `validateConfigObjectWithPlugins` → `writeConfigFile`) |
| `src/telegram/settings-auth.ts` (120 LOC) | Shared auth decision function used by both the `/settings` command and `cfg_*` callback paths, guaranteeing auth parity |
| `src/telegram/bot-native-commands.ts` | `/settings` command handler registered as standalone `bot.command("settings", ...)` with auth, DM-only guard, and `configWrites` guard |
| `src/telegram/bot-handlers.ts` | `cfg_` callback dispatch before `inlineButtonsScope` gates, using real callback message context for thread-aware auth |
| `src/auto-reply/commands-registry.data.ts` | Register `settings` as a Telegram-only native management command (`providers: ["telegram"]`) |

### Callback data encoding

Following the `mdl_` pattern from `model-buttons.ts`:

```
cfg_menu                  → main settings menu
cfg_s_{setting}           → submenu (dmpol, grppol, stream, allow)
cfg_v_{setting}_{value}   → set value (e.g. cfg_v_dmpol_open)
```

### Guards

- **DM-only**: groups get an explicit "Settings is only available in DMs" reply
- **Auth**: shared `resolveSettingsAuthDecision` enforcing the full command-level auth chain (requireTopic, per-DM/topic allowFrom overrides, `commands.allowFrom`, `ownerAllowFrom`)
- **Fresh config per callback**: every button press re-reads config from disk — no stale registration-time state
- **configWrites**: respects `channels.telegram.configWrites: false` (both command and callbacks)
- **nativeEnabled**: `/settings` handler only registers when native commands are enabled
- **Account-scoped**: default account writes to `channels.telegram.{key}`, named accounts to `channels.telegram.accounts.{id}.{key}`

### Callback auth hardening

Settings callbacks mutate config, so they require stricter auth than read-only inline buttons. The callback path was hardened across multiple review rounds to reach full parity with the `/settings` command auth:

- Callback handler runs **before** `inlineButtonsScope` gates (settings has its own auth and shouldn't be blocked by `scope=off` or `scope=group`)
- Uses the **actual callback message context** (`isForum`, `messageThreadId`) for thread-aware auth — `requireTopic`, per-topic `allowFrom`, and per-DM `dmPolicy` overrides are all enforced
- Auth logic extracted into `resolveSettingsAuthDecision` (shared between command and callback paths) to eliminate parity drift
- Only uses fresh config values — no fallback to stale registration-time `allowFrom`
- Enforces `commands.allowFrom`, `commands.ownerAllowFrom`, and `useAccessGroups` identically to the command path via `resolveCommandAuthorizedFromAuthorizers` + `resolveCommandAuthorization`

### Settings covered

| Key | Config path | Valid values |
|-----|------------|--------------|
| `dmpol` | `dmPolicy` | pairing, allowlist, open, disabled |
| `grppol` | `groupPolicy` | open, allowlist, disabled |
| `stream` | `streaming` | off, partial, block |

The `progress` streaming alias is hidden (maps to `partial`). Legacy boolean streaming values are normalized for display.

## Scope boundaries

- Account-level settings only (no per-group/per-topic overrides in v1)
- Allowlist is read-only in the panel (add/remove via `/allowlist` text commands)
- `requireMention` is intentionally excluded — it's a per-group setting, not account-level
- No gateway restart needed — `loadConfig()` re-reads from disk on next request

## Related issues

- Partially addresses #16062 — improves Telegram chat UX settings for AI-agent conversations
- Related to #22508 — Telegram inline buttons toast/alert text in `answerCallbackQuery` (this PR uses chat replies for feedback since `answerCallbackQuery` is called immediately before parsing)

target.title: feat: add concurrent execution support for sessions_send_concurrent tool
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds concurrent execution support for the `sessions_send_concurrent` tool with full agent-to-agent (A2A) communication support. The tool enables concurrent messaging to 1-20 agent sessions with streaming responses and real-time progress updates, achieving 3-8x performance improvement compared to serial execution.

## Changes

### Core Gateway Changes
- **src/gateway/server-lanes.ts**: Added `CommandLane.Nested` concurrency support using `resolveAgentMaxConcurrent(cfg)` (default 16)
- **src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.ts**: Added `CommandLane.Nested` concurrency support for config hot reload

### New Tool Implementation
- **src/agents/tools/sessions-send-concurrent-tool.ts**: New tool for concurrent agent messaging (1-20 targets)
  - Streaming responses with real-time progress updates via `onUpdate` callback
  - Fire-and-forget mode (timeoutSeconds: 0) for async execution
  - Independent timeout configuration per target
  - Session visibility guards and sandboxed mode support
  - Comprehensive error isolation with wrapped promises

- **src/agents/tools/sessions-send-concurrent-a2a.ts**: Complete A2A flow implementation
  - Ping-pong loop support (same conditions as `sessions_send`)
  - Announce step support (same conditions as `sessions_send`)
  - Fire-and-forget async background execution
  - Comprehensive error handling and logging

- **src/agents/tools/sessions-send-concurrent-tool.test.ts**: Complete test suite
  - 9 test cases covering parameter validation, schema structure, and progress updates
  - All tests passing

### Documentation
- **docs/tools/sessions-send-concurrent.md**: Complete tool documentation (641 lines)
  - Feature descriptions and parameter documentation
  - 5 comprehensive usage examples
  - Performance comparison tables
  - Agent-to-Agent communication section
  - Best practices and configuration guide
  - Error handling documentation
  - Technical implementation details

### Tool Registration
- **src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts**: Tool registration for `sessions_send_concurrent`
- **src/agents/tool-catalog.ts**: Tool catalog updates

## Performance Impact

| Scenario | Serial Execution | Concurrent Execution | Improvement |
|----------|-----------------|---------------------|-------------|
| 4 targets | ~200s | ~60s | **3.3x** |
| 16 targets | ~470s | ~60s | **7.8x** |

**With A2A overhead**: Still 3-5x faster than serial `sessions_send` calls

## Technical Details

### Background
`CommandLane.Nested` was not configured with a concurrency limit, defaulting to 1, causing all A2A messages to execute serially.

### Implementation
- Set `CommandLane.Nested` concurrency to `resolveAgentMaxConcurrent(cfg)` (default 16)
- Implemented complete A2A flow (ping-pong + announce) identical to `sessions_send`
- Uses `Promise.allSettled` for error isolation
- Provides real-time progress updates via streaming callbacks

### Configuration
- Concurrency: `agents.defaults.maxConcurrent` in `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` (default 16)
- Ping-pong: `session.agentToAgent.maxPingPongTurns` (default 5)
- Session visibility: `tools.sessions.visibility` (default: "created" for sandboxed mode)

## Testing

1. ✅ Code compiles without errors
2. ✅ Gateway restarts successfully
3. ✅ All 9 test cases passing
4. ✅ Code formatted with oxfmt
5. ✅ Code comments in English
6. ⏳ Manual testing recommended: Test `sessions_send_concurrent` with 4-16 concurrent targets
7. ⏳ Integration testing: Verify concurrent execution with real agent workflows

## Documentation

- Complete tool documentation added in `docs/tools/sessions-send-concurrent.md`
- Code comments updated to English
- Includes usage examples, best practices, and configuration guide
- A2A behavior fully documented with examples

## API Compatibility

**No breaking changes**: Tool parameters remain unchanged

**New result field**: `delivery` object added to results for A2A flow status

```json
{
  "sessionKey": "agent:coder:main",
  "displayKey": "agent:coder:main",
  "status": "ok",
  "reply": "Agent response",
  "runId": "uuid",
  "completedAt": 1234567890,
  "delivery": {
    "status": "pending",
    "mode": "announce"
  }
}
```

## Checklist

- [x] Code compiles without errors
- [x] Gateway restarts successfully
- [x] All tests passing (9/9)
- [x] Code formatted with oxfmt
- [x] Code comments in English
- [x] Documentation in English

- [x] A2A behavior consistent with `sessions_send`
- [ ] Manual testing completed (optional, recommended for validation)
- [ ] Integration testing completed (optional, CI/CD will run automated tests)

target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw can report health and channel errors, but there was no external rescue loop that stays alive when the gateway itself is unhealthy.
- Why it matters: in-process recovery dies with the same process, so failures that stop replies had to be diagnosed and handed off manually.
- What changed: added an external rescue watchdog script, a Claude Code runner, systemd user service/timer templates, and setup docs.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no core gateway runtime behavior, no automatic commit/push flow, and no bundled launchd plist.

- Added `scripts/rescue-watchdog.sh` to detect `openclaw health --json` failures and channel `lastError` snapshots, capture incident bundles, de-duplicate repeats, optionally notify, and optionally invoke a rescue runner.
- Added `scripts/claude-rescue-runner.sh` as a minimal Claude Code handoff runner for captured incidents.
- Added `scripts/systemd/openclaw-rescue-watchdog.{service,timer}` templates.
- Added `docs/automation/rescue-watchdog.md` and linked it from auth monitoring docs.

## Security Impact (required)

- Data access scope changed? (`Yes`)
